title: Advancing fronts for the thin-film equation with null slip and repulsive potentials: the case of partial wetting
summary: For negative values of the spreading coefficient (that is, in the so-called ``partial wetting'' regime), we prove that the thin-film equation with zero slip and repulsive potentials $P$ of the form $P(h)\approx h^{1-m}$ as $h\to 0$, $m>1$, admits for any positive speed a one-parameter family of travelling-wave solutions with a contact line and (as in standard slippage models) a logarithmically-corrected linear behaviour as $h\to +\infty$. These waves have locally finite rate of dissipation for any $m>1$ and locally finite energy for any $m\in (1,3)$. The result thus confirms that mildly repulsive potentials effectively resolve the no-slip paradox. The family is parametrized by a thermodynamically consistent contact-line condition which reduces to the classical fixed microscopic contact-angle one if $P\equiv 0$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that the thin-film equation with zero slip and repulsive potentials admits traveling-wave solutions with a contact line for any positive speed in partial wetting regimes, resolving the no-slip paradox for mildly repulsive potentials (m>1 for dissipation, m∈(1,3) for energy).
AI.motivation: To resolve the no-slip paradox in thin-film equations by showing that mildly repulsive potentials can eliminate the singularity at the contact line without requiring slip conditions.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis proving existence of one-parameter family of traveling-wave solutions for the thin-film equation with repulsive potentials P(h)~h^{1-m} (m>1) in the partial wetting regime (negative spreading coefficient).
AI.result: For any m>1, traveling-wave solutions exist with locally finite dissipation; for m∈(1,3), they have locally finite energy. The contact-line condition reduces to classical fixed microscopic contact-angle when P≡0.
AI.conclusion: Mildly repulsive potentials effectively resolve the no-slip paradox, as they enable well-behaved contact-line dynamics without requiring slip models.

title: QVaR: a Quantum Variational Regularization method for Linear Inverse Problems
summary: We present a tailored framework for solving regularized linear inverse problems using quantum optimization methods. By discretizing the solution space and encoding data fidelity and regularization terms into quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) models, we formulate both Tikhonov- and sparsity-promoting regularized inverse problems within a unified quantum optimization framework. We further introduce a notion of quantum sensitivity that characterizes the effect of perturbations arising from approximate quantum evolution and discretization. We derive bounds relating these perturbations to stability properties of the underlying inverse problem, thereby establishing a theoretical connection between quantum solution variability and classical notions of ill-posedness. The framework is extended to variational inverse problems through wavelet-based representations and complemented by reduced-order modeling strategies in both parameter and Hamiltonian spaces to mitigate current hardware limitations. Numerical experiments on simulated and physical quantum hardware indicate that the resulting low-energy solution distributions retain information about the underlying inverse problem, while also revealing the limitations imposed by finite-time evolution, discretization, and hardware noise.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a quantum optimization framework for solving regularized linear inverse problems (Tikhonov and sparsity-promoting) using QUBO models, introduces quantum sensitivity to characterize perturbation effects, extends to variational problems via wavelets, and uses reduced-order modeling to address hardware limits. Experiments on simulated and physical quantum hardware show promise but reveal constraints from finite-time evolution, discretization, and noise.
AI.motivation: To bridge quantum computing and classical inverse problems by providing a unified QUBO-based framework that handles both Tikhonov and sparsity-promoting regularization, while establishing theoretical connections between quantum solution variability and classical ill-posedness.
AI.method: Discretizes solution space, encodes data fidelity and regularization into QUBO models; introduces quantum sensitivity to link perturbations to stability; extends to variational inverse problems via wavelet representations; uses reduced-order modeling in parameter and Hamiltonian spaces to mitigate hardware limitations.
AI.result: Low-energy solution distributions from quantum hardware retain information about the inverse problem, but finite-time evolution, discretization, and hardware noise impose significant limitations.
AI.conclusion: Quantum optimization can be applied to regularized inverse problems, but practical deployment requires addressing hardware noise and evolution constraints; the proposed framework and sensitivity analysis provide a theoretical foundation for further development.

title: Semi-local Floquet theory for active azimuthal magnetic modulation of Hall-thruster high-frequency instabilities
summary: A semi-local Floquet extension of a uniform-field kinetic electron drift instability (EDI) dispersion relation is developed to assess prescribed azimuthal magnetic-field modulation as a linear pre-screening tool for Hallthruster high-frequency instabilities. The uniform kinetic response is used as a local spectral kernel, while a sinusoidal magnetic modulation couples Floquet sidebands and replaces the scalar dispersion condition by a finite matrix dispersion problem. The numerical procedure combines scalar uniform-field predictors, determinant correction, singular-value diagnostics, sideband-weight analysis, and truncation checks. Because a single Floquet root contains multiple physical wave numbers, stability is assessed with the upper growth envelope over the Bloch zone rather than with an individual projected azimuthal wave-number branch. Parameter scans over modulation wavelength and amplitude show that sinusoidal azimuthal magnetic modulation broadens the coupled spectrum and redistributes unstable growth among low-wave-number modified-two-stream-like and cyclotron-resonant ranges. Some long-wavelength, moderate-to-large-amplitude cases reduce integrated positive growth measures, but these reductions are not accompanied by robust suppression of the peak growth envelope. No tested case produces a finite stable Bloch interval. Within the present cold-ion semi-local Floquet model, prescribed azimuthal magnetic modulation is therefore better interpreted as a spectral-redistribution mechanism than as a robust linear stabilization mechanism by itself.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a semi-local Floquet model to assess azimuthal magnetic-field modulation as a linear pre-screening tool for Hall thruster instabilities. The model shows that modulation broadens the spectrum and redistributes growth but does not produce robust linear stabilization or stable Bloch intervals.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether prescribed azimuthal magnetic-field modulation can serve as a linear pre-screening tool for mitigating high-frequency instabilities in Hall thrusters.
AI.method: A semi-local Floquet extension of a uniform-field EDI dispersion relation is developed. The uniform kinetic response serves as a local spectral kernel; sinusoidal magnetic modulation couples Floquet sidebands; the dispersion condition becomes a finite matrix problem solved via scalar predictors, determinant correction, SVD, sideband-weight analysis, and truncation checks.
AI.result: Magnetic modulation broadens the coupled spectrum and redistributes unstable growth between low-wave-number MTS-like and cyclotron-resonant ranges. Some long-wavelength, large-amplitude cases reduce integrated growth but do not suppress the peak growth envelope. No case yields a finite stable Bloch interval.
AI.conclusion: Prescribed azimuthal magnetic modulation acts as a spectral-redistribution mechanism rather than a robust linear stabilization method by itself within the cold-ion semi-local Floquet model.

title: Inverse Identification of Surface Elastic Parameters in Soft Solids Using GA-ANN Surrogate Model
summary: Surface elasticity plays a crucial role in the mechanics of soft solids at the scale of micrometers and may become significant even at the scale of millimeters in exceptional cases. However, despite the efforts made for the identification of surface elastic parameters, accurately determining these values remains challenging due to the complex interplay with bulk elasticity and nonlinearity. To address this issue, a novel GA-based optimization framework is developed by employing an ANN-based surrogate forward model for efficient identification of surface parameters from the force-displacement response of a cylindrical specimen. The ANN is trained on force-displacement data of a soft cylindrical specimen, generated by nonlinear FE model predictions incorporating model elastic surfaces. The data used for training is generated for non-dimensional values of surface tension in the range of 0-5 and surface shear modulus in the range of 0-50. The accuracy and reliability of the trained ANN are established. The key novelty lies in replacing analytical forward models, which rely on idealized boundary conditions, with a data-driven surrogate trained on FE simulations under realistic constraints. The parameter identification is performed for a number of surface parameter sets using numerically generated force-displacement data as well as for noisy data obtained by adding 5% error in simulated data. The maximum error in identified values of parameters in all the cases is less than 8%. Repeatability and uncertainty analyses based on multiple noisy realizations further demonstrate the robustness of the approach, yielding narrow confidence intervals for the predicted parameters. The proposed framework provides a novel, efficient, and robust alternative to FE-based inverse identification of surface parameters, particularly for experimentally relevant boundary conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a GA-based optimization framework with an ANN surrogate model trained on nonlinear FE simulations to efficiently identify surface elastic parameters (surface tension and shear modulus) from force-displacement data of soft cylindrical specimens, achieving under 8% error and demonstrating robustness against noise.
AI.motivation: Surface elasticity significantly affects soft solids at small scales, but accurately determining surface elastic parameters is challenging due to complex interactions with bulk elasticity and nonlinearity. Existing analytical forward models rely on idealized boundary conditions that limit practical applicability.
AI.method: A GA-based optimization framework is combined with an ANN surrogate forward model trained on nonlinear FE simulation data (surface tension 0-5, shear modulus 0-50) to identify surface parameters from force-displacement responses. The ANN replaces traditional analytical forward models by learning from FE simulations under realistic constraints.
AI.result: Maximum identification error < 8% across all parameter sets, including with 5% added noise. Repeatability and uncertainty analyses with multiple noisy realizations yield narrow confidence intervals, demonstrating robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed data-driven surrogate framework offers an efficient, robust alternative to FE-based inverse identification of surface parameters, especially for experimentally relevant boundary conditions where analytical models fall short.

title: Determination of the long-time dynamics for the 2D Keller-Segel equation at critical mass
summary: We consider the parabolic-elliptic Keller-Segel equation in two dimensions on the whole space. We prove that for arbitrary initial data with critical mass $8π$ and finite second momentum, all solutions have the same universal behaviour. They are globally defined and, asymptotically for large times, they converge to a renormalized stationary state of the equation which concentrates around the center of mass of the solution at a universal logarithmic-in-time scale. Our result holds for general solutions without symmetry assumptions, and we furthermore provide explicit convergence rates. In the radial case, by combining our result with previous ones (by Blanchet-Dolbeault-Perthame, Mizoguchi, and related works), this achieves a complete classification of the possible dynamics: for subcritical masses solutions converge to a self-similar expander, at the critical mass they concentrate a stationary state in infinite time at the aforementioned universal scale, and for supercritical masses they blow up in finite time by type II concentration of a stationary state at another universal scale. Our proof starts with soliton resolution, then controls the motion of the stationary state and the remainder in new spaces, and devises new techniques for the multi-scale linearized analysis, which eventually enables the stability and modulation analysis around an approximate solution.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that all 2D parabolic-elliptic Keller-Segel equations with critical mass 8π and finite second momentum converge universally to a renormalized stationary state, achieving a complete classification of dynamics across subcritical, critical, and supercritical mass regimes.
AI.motivation: To achieve a complete classification of possible dynamics for the Keller-Segel equation across all mass regimes (subcritical, critical, supercritical), and to prove universal asymptotic behavior at the critical mass without symmetry assumptions.
AI.method: The proof begins with soliton resolution, controls the motion of the stationary state and remainder in new function spaces, and develops novel multi-scale linearized analysis techniques for stability and modulation around an approximate solution.
AI.result: For critical mass 8π, all solutions converge to a renormalized stationary state at a universal logarithmic-in-time scale around the center of mass. Explicit convergence rates are provided. Combined with prior results, this completes the full dynamical classification.
AI.conclusion: The dynamics are fully classified: subcritical masses → self-similar expander, critical mass → stationary state concentration in infinite time at universal scale, supercritical masses → finite-time type II blow-up via stationary state concentration at another universal scale.

title: An FFT-based solver with general boundary conditions for stationary diffusion problems based on Chebyshev collocation
summary:
An efficient and robust FFT-based solver is proposed for diffusion-type problems with general Neumann and Dirichlet boundary conditions, based on a Chebyshev collocation framework. The method combines Chebyshev polynomial approximations with FFT-based operators to provide a matrix-free implementation of the discrete differential operator at the Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto points. The linear system of equations resulting from the Chebyshev discretization is solved using LGMRES. To overcome convergence problems on fine grids, a hierarchical refinement strategy based on modal prolongation is proposed, enabling the solution of very large 3D problems. The methodology is applicable to homogeneous and heterogeneous domains, as well as to linear and nonlinear constitutive equations.
  The accuracy of the proposed method is analyzed by solving the Poisson equation in homogeneous 1D and 3D domains with general boundary conditions, using manufactured analytical solutions as references. Convergence to the analytical solution is achieved in a few iterations, with smaller errors than those obtained using DCT/DST approaches. Discretizations of up to $256^3$ are achieved thanks to the hierarchical refinement strategy. In the case of heterogeneous domains, the accuracy and efficiency obtained are similar to those of a standard periodic FFT approach. It is found that the computational complexity of the method preserves the FFT scaling, of order $n\log n$, in all the cases studied.

AI.tldr: A Chebyshev collocation FFT-based solver for diffusion-type problems with Neumann/Dirichlet BCs, using LGMRES and hierarchical refinement to achieve FFT-scaling complexity.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient and robust FFT-based solver for diffusion problems with general (non-periodic) boundary conditions, addressing convergence issues on fine grids.
AI.method: Combines Chebyshev polynomial approximations with FFT-based operators at Chebyshev-Gauss-Lobatto points, uses LGMRES solver, and employs a hierarchical refinement strategy with modal prolongation for large 3D problems.
AI.result: Converges in few iterations, smaller errors than DCT/DST approaches, handles up to 256^3 grids, preserves O(n log n) FFT complexity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is accurate and efficient for both homogeneous and heterogeneous domains with general BCs, maintaining FFT scaling across all tested cases.

title: Reconnection-induced electron energization in magnetospheric Kelvin-Helmholtz dynamics
summary: The Kelvin-Helmholtz instability (KHI) is a major driver of multiscale plasma dynamics at velocity shear layers, where it can promote the formation of current sheets and the onset of magnetic reconnection as well as drive plasma energization. While recent kinetic studies have shown efficient electron heating during nonlinear KH evolution, the connection between reconnection dynamics and localized electron energization is still not fully understood. We investigate this link using two-dimensional fully kinetic simulations of KHI developing in a double-periodic system with two velocity shear layers and a uniform guide field, initialized from a finite-Larmor-radius equilibrium. During the nonlinear stage, initially coherent vortices evolve into layers populated by fragmented current sheets displaying reconnection activity. The global energetics reveal species-dependent energization pathways. Ions act as the primary energy reservoir, transferring energy to the electromagnetic fields, while electrons receive the dominant net positive energy input. Electron energization is strongly anisotropic ($T_{\parallel,e} > T_{\perp,e}$) and localized within intermittent current sheets associated with enhanced field-particle energy exchange and elevated agyrotropy. These regions also show the development of suprathermal tails in the electron energy distributions, providing evidence for nonthermal electron energization. Despite opposite vorticity orientations, the two shear layers exhibit similar statistical behavior. Together, these results establish a direct connection between reconnection-associated current structures and localized electron energization in collisionless KHI dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper uses 2D kinetic simulations to show that in the Kelvin-Helmholtz instability, electrons are energized anisotropically (T∥ > T⊥) within fragmented current sheets undergoing reconnection, establishing a direct link between reconnection dynamics and localized nonthermal electron energization.
AI.motivation: While prior work showed efficient electron heating during nonlinear KH evolution, the direct connection between reconnection dynamics and localized electron energization remains poorly understood.
AI.method: Two-dimensional fully kinetic simulations of KHI in a double-periodic system with two velocity shear layers and a uniform guide field, initialized from a finite-Larmor-radius equilibrium.
AI.result: Ions transfer energy to electromagnetic fields while electrons receive net positive energy; electron energization is anisotropic (T∥,e > T⊥,e) and localized in current sheets with reconnection activity, producing suprathermal tails; both shear layers show similar statistical behavior despite opposite vorticities.
AI.conclusion: There is a direct connection between reconnection-associated current structures and localized nonthermal electron energization in collisionless KHI dynamics.

title: Properties of the matrix functions arising in exponential integrators with applications to stochastic simulations of PDEs
summary: The matrix exponential and the closely related matrix phi-functions play a fundamental role in the solution of first-order systems of ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In particular, they appear in exact solutions of certain linear ODE systems, and in exponential integrators, a class of explicit time discretisation methods for computing approximate solutions of stiff semi-linear ODE systems. Fundamental properties of the matrix exponential include that it is nonnegative if the matrix is essentially nonnegative and stochastic if the matrix is a transition-rate matrix. In this paper, we study related properties for the matrix phi-functions. Using these properties, we then provide insights into deterministic solutions of linear and semi-linear ODE systems and outline how such solutions can be used to generate stochastic simulations to quantify variability in model predictions. The paper concludes with some illustrative examples exploring the theory for some ODE systems arising from spatial discretisation of PDEs of diffusion, advection-diffusion, Fisher-KPP and Allen-Cahn type.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes nonnegativity and stochasticity properties of matrix phi-functions (extending known properties of matrix exponentials) and uses them to connect deterministic ODE solutions with stochastic simulations, illustrated on diffusion, advection-diffusion, Fisher-KPP, and Allen-Cahn PDEs.
AI.motivation: To extend the known nonnegativity and stochasticity properties of the matrix exponential to matrix phi-functions, and to leverage these properties for linking deterministic ODE solutions to stochastic simulations for quantifying variability.
AI.method: The authors study structural properties of matrix phi-functions (analogous to those of the matrix exponential), then apply these properties to linear and semi-linear ODE systems, connecting deterministic solutions to stochastic simulation frameworks.
AI.result: Matrix phi-functions inherit nonnegativity (when the matrix is essentially nonnegative) and stochasticity (when the matrix is a transition-rate matrix) properties similar to the matrix exponential, enabling stochastic interpretation of deterministic ODE solutions.
AI.conclusion: The established properties of matrix phi-functions bridge deterministic ODE solutions and stochastic simulations, demonstrated on PDE-derived ODE systems (diffusion, advection-diffusion, Fisher-KPP, Allen-Cahn).

title: Classification of the dynamics of radial solutions to the 2D parabolic-elliptic Keller-Segel System
summary: This note gives a complete classification of the asymptotic behavior of radial solutions to the two-dimensional parabolic-elliptic Keller-Segel system on the whole space, for general initial data in the large. We review previous separate results, and unify them within a single classification framework. Depending on the mass, the flow exhibits three distinct asymptotic regimes. For a subcritical mass, solutions converge toward the unique self-similar expander of same mass. At the critical mass $8π$, solutions concentrate in infinite time around the stationary state with a universal logarithmic rate. The determination of this behaviour was the last missing step for achieving a complete radial classification, and we prove it in a companion paper (in fact without radial assumption). For a supercritical mass, solutions undergo type II finite-time blow-up with an explicit universal asymptotic rate and the stationary state as profile. This trichotomy holds for all radial initial data with finite second momentum. For non-radial data or infinite second momentum, it is known that other dynamics can be possible; for each of these three universal regimes we review the known results showing how they persist.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a complete classification of asymptotic behaviors for radial solutions of the 2D parabolic-elliptic Keller-Segel system, identifying three distinct regimes: subcritical mass (convergence to self-similar expander), critical mass 8π (infinite-time concentration with logarithmic rate), and supercritical mass (finite-time type II blow-up).
AI.motivation: To achieve a complete and unified classification of the asymptotic behavior of radial solutions to the Keller-Segel system, bridging previous separate results and filling the last missing gap (critical mass case).
AI.method: The paper reviews and synthesizes prior results, proving the critical mass behavior in a companion paper, and unifies all three regimes (subcritical, critical, supercritical) within a single classification framework.
AI.result: A trichotomy of asymptotic regimes is established for all radial initial data with finite second momentum: (1) subcritical mass → self-similar expander, (2) critical mass 8π → infinite-time concentration with universal logarithmic rate, (3) supercritical mass → finite-time type II blow-up with explicit universal rate.
AI.conclusion: The complete radial classification is achieved, and the three universal regimes are shown to persist beyond the radial case and finite second momentum assumptions, though other dynamics are possible in those broader settings.

title: An Aubin-Nitsche Lemma for a positivity-preserving finite element method
summary:
In this work we prove an Aubin-Nitsche Lemma for a positivity-preserving discretisation of an elliptic
  problem. Due to the nonlinearity of the discretisation, the result requires as a first step
  the proposal of a linearised adjoint problem that can be linked to the method of
  choice by appropriately selecting weights. This linearised adjoint problem, together
  with a regularity result, allow the proof of an optimal-order error estimate in the $L^2$-norm of
  the error.

AI.tldr: This paper proves an Aubin-Nitsche Lemma for a positivity-preserving discretisation of elliptic problems, establishing optimal-order L² error estimates through a linearised adjoint problem with carefully selected weights.
AI.motivation: To obtain optimal-order L² error estimates for nonlinear positivity-preserving discretisations of elliptic problems, which requires extending classical Aubin-Nitsche techniques to handle the nonlinearity.
AI.method: Proposes a linearised adjoint problem linked to the discretisation via appropriately selected weights, combined with regularity results to prove the Aubin-Nitsche Lemma.
AI.result: Proof of an optimal-order error estimate in the L²-norm of the error for positivity-preserving discretisations of elliptic problems.
AI.conclusion: The linearised adjoint approach with suitable weights successfully extends the Aubin-Nitsche framework to nonlinear positivity-preserving discretisations, yielding optimal convergence rates.

title: Geometric numerical discretization of electromagnetic quasineutral models
summary: In this work, the geometric electromagnetic Particle-in-Cell (PIC) framework, GEMPICX, is extended to solve the quasineutral, fully kinetic Vlasov-Maxwell equations on dual grids using mimetic finite differences. The discrete action principle is derived, taking into account the duality between the grids. The temporal derivative of the electric field does not directly appear in the dynamical system for the quasineutral model. Hence, a discretized curl-curl equation is used to implicitly obtain the electric field at every time-step. This also circumvents the need to obtain electric potentials. A Lagrange multiplier is used to maintain the discretized divergence of the current density at machine zero.

AI.tldr: Extends GEMPICX to solve quasineutral Vlasov-Maxwell equations on dual grids using mimetic finite differences, with implicit electric field update via curl-curl equation and Lagrange multiplier for current divergence.
AI.motivation: To extend the GEMPICX framework to handle quasineutral plasma models, removing the need for explicit electric potential calculation while maintaining numerical accuracy.
AI.method: Derives a discrete action principle on dual grids using mimetic finite differences; uses a discretized curl-curl equation to implicitly compute the electric field; employs a Lagrange multiplier to enforce divergence-free current density.
AI.result: Achieves quasineutral simulation without electric potentials; maintains current divergence at machine zero via Lagrange multiplier.
AI.conclusion: Provides a robust mimetic finite difference approach for quasineutral Vlasov-Maxwell simulations within the GEMPICX framework.

title: Parallelized contraction of tensor trains or matrix product operators
summary: Tensor Trains (TT), also known as Matrix Product States (MPS) and Matrix Product Operators (MPO), provide a compact and structured representation for high-dimensional data and operators. One of the most expensive manipulations involving tensor trains is the contraction of two MPOs. A popular and accurate method for mitigating this cost is the fit algorithm. However, it is still comparatively costly since it involves 2-site updates. Moreover, the parallelization of the fit algorithm when used for MPO-MPO contractions has received comparatively little attention. In this work, we present two strategies for accelerating the fit algorithm, usable in combination: (1) We use MPI-based distributed-memory parallelization tailored for MPO-MPO contractions, employing one of two MPO gauge choices: (1a) the inverse canonical gauge, which yields near-ideal parallelization speedup across all problem sizes; and (1b) the site-canonical gauge, which avoids the inversion of singular values but requires extra computations to ensure global consistency, thus yielding excellent parallelization speedup only for large problems requiring several sweeps before convergence. (2) We use randomized projections to reduce the cost of local updates from 2-site to 1-site costs while retaining 2-site accuracy, and to speed up contractions of environment tensors with MPO tensors.

AI.tldr: The paper presents two acceleration strategies for MPO-MPO contractions using tensor trains: MPI-based distributed-memory parallelization with two gauge choices (inverse canonical and site-canonical), and randomized projections to reduce local update costs from 2-site to 1-site while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: MPO-MPO contractions are expensive operations in tensor train computations, and the fit algorithm, while accurate, remains costly due to 2-site updates and lacks efficient parallelization strategies for this specific task.
AI.method: Two strategies: (1) MPI-based distributed-memory parallelization with (1a) inverse canonical gauge for near-ideal speedup, or (1b) site-canonical gauge to avoid singular value inversion; (2) Randomized projections to reduce 2-site costs to 1-site costs while maintaining 2-site accuracy.
AI.result: Inverse canonical gauge achieves near-ideal parallelization speedup across all problem sizes; site-canonical gauge works well only for large problems needing multiple sweeps. Randomized projections reduce local update costs while retaining accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed combined strategies significantly accelerate MPO-MPO contractions by addressing both computational cost and parallelization efficiency in the fit algorithm.

title: Local Energy Decay for Non-Stationary Damped Wave Operators
summary: The paper establishes integrated local energy decay (ILED) estimates for the damped wave equation on certain non-stationary spacetimes. The main technical result is a high frequency estimate that holds in great generality, provided that null geodesics trapped in a compact region are sufficiently damped.

AI.tldr: The paper proves integrated local energy decay estimates for damped wave equations on non-stationary spacetimes, with the key result being a high-frequency estimate that works broadly as long as null geodesics trapped in a compact region are sufficiently damped.
AI.motivation: To establish ILED estimates for damped wave equations on non-stationary spacetimes, addressing the challenge of trapped null geodesics.
AI.method: A high-frequency estimate approach that applies under the condition that null geodesics trapped in a compact region receive sufficient damping.
AI.result: Integrated local energy decay estimates hold in great generality for non-stationary spacetimes with sufficient damping of trapped null geodesics.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a general framework for ILED estimates on non-stationary spacetimes, with the damping condition on trapped null geodesics being the key requirement.

title: A spherical harmonic pseudo-spectral approach to mean curvature flow of surfaces with spherical topology
summary: We propose and analyze a spherical harmonic pseudo-spectral method for the mean curvature flow of closed surfaces with spherical topology. The evolving surface is represented by a global parametrization over the unit sphere, and the continuous weak formulation underlying Dziuk's method [G. Dziuk, Numer. Math., 1991] is discretized in a finite-dimensional space of spherical harmonics. By explicitly taking into account quadrature errors on the autonomously evolving numerical surface, we prove exponential convergence of the position error under the assumption that the initial global parametrization is analytic. The convergence analysis developed herein is general and could apply to other moving-domain and geometric evolution problems. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical result.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a spherical harmonic pseudo-spectral method for mean curvature flow of closed surfaces with spherical topology, proving exponential convergence for analytic initial parametrizations, with numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To develop a high-accuracy numerical method for mean curvature flow that achieves exponential convergence, overcoming limitations of traditional finite element approaches.
AI.method: Global parametrization over unit sphere + spherical harmonic discretization of Dziuk's weak formulation + explicit quadrature error analysis for evolving surfaces.
AI.result: Proved exponential convergence of position error under analytic initial parametrization; numerical experiments confirm theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectral method achieves exponential convergence for mean curvature flow, and the general analysis framework applies to other moving-domain/geometric evolution problems.

title: Geometric numerical discretization of a quasineutral hybrid model of drift-kinetic electrons and fully kinetic ions
summary: We extend the geometric electromagnetic particle-in-cell (PIC) framework, GEMPICX, to solve the quasineutral hybrid Vlasov-Maxwell equations with drift-kinetic electrons and fully kinetic ions. A structure-preserving finite difference method that employs dual grids is used. The discrete action principle for the hybrid model is derived, using the dual nature of the grids. The dynamical system for this hybrid quasineutral model does not explicitly involve the temporal evolution term for the electric field. A curl-curl equation is therefore used to implicitly obtain the component of the electric field that is parallel to the background magnetic field, at every timestep. The perpendicular component of the electric field is obtained using the quasineutral Ampere's equation without the displacement current, combined with the definition of the current in the drift-kinetic model. The discretized versions of the electric field equations are large, sparse linear systems. A fully explicit time-stepping scheme as well as two implicit-explicit (IMEX) schemes are tested. The numerical model is validated by verifying the various waves obtained from the dispersion relation.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the GEMPICX framework to solve hybrid Vlasov-Maxwell equations with drift-kinetic electrons and fully kinetic ions, using structure-preserving finite difference methods on dual grids and IMEX time-stepping schemes.
AI.motivation: To extend the geometric electromagnetic PIC framework (GEMPICX) for hybrid models that combine drift-kinetic electrons and fully kinetic ions, addressing the quasineutral regime where the electric field evolution is not explicitly given.
AI.method: Structure-preserving finite difference method using dual grids; discrete action principle for the hybrid model; implicit solution of parallel electric field via curl-curl equation; perpendicular field via quasineutral Ampere's equation; explicit and IMEX time-stepping schemes.
AI.result: The discretized electric field equations form large sparse linear systems; numerical model validated by verifying dispersion relation waves.
AI.conclusion: The extended GEMPICX framework successfully handles the quasineutral hybrid model with structure preservation, and both explicit and IMEX schemes are viable for time integration.

title: NektarIR: A Domain-Specific Compiler for High-Order Finite Element Operations on Heterogeneous Hardware
summary: Modern high performance computing (HPC) applications must target heterogeneous hardware. This requires significant work to ensure domain specific implementations translate to highly performant kernels across a range hardware types and vendors, each requiring bespoke optimization to make use of the specific target architecture. Through the development of a domain specific compiler built with the multi-level intermediate representations (MLIR) project, one can express a high-level, close to the specific domain, abstraction that is progressively lowered to a low, close to metal, abstraction. At each intermediate representation (IR), appropriate optimizations can be applied without costly analysis due to the knowledge embedded in the domain specific IRs. We apply this method to the construction of discrete differential operators for use in spectral/hp element method solvers for computational fluid dynamics (CFD). Here, the performance is driven by a small set of common finite element operators that are composed to create kernels for the discrete differential operators used to solve weak partial differential equations. We create our own MLIR dialect to represent these operators and implement a bespoke lowering pipeline to facilitate the just-in-time compilation of these kernels for both CPU and GPU architecture and illustrate performance comparisons with the Nektar++ spectral/hp element framework.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an MLIR-based domain-specific compiler for building high-performance discrete differential operators on heterogeneous hardware (CPUs and GPUs), targeting spectral/hp element method CFD solvers, with performance comparisons to Nektar++.
AI.motivation: Modern HPC applications must target heterogeneous hardware, requiring significant bespoke optimization for each architecture. The goal is to reduce this effort by using domain-specific compilation to automatically generate performant kernels across different hardware types.
AI.method: A domain-specific compiler is built using MLIR, creating a custom MLIR dialect to represent finite element operators. A bespoke lowering pipeline progressively lowers high-level domain abstractions to low-level, hardware-specific code, enabling just-in-time compilation for both CPU and GPU architectures.
AI.result: The approach successfully produces performant kernels for both CPU and GPU architectures, with performance comparisons made against the Nektar++ spectral/hp element framework.
AI.conclusion: Using MLIR-based domain-specific compilation is an effective method for generating optimized kernels for heterogeneous HPC systems, reducing the need for manual, hardware-specific optimization while maintaining high performance.

title: A Decay Estimate For The Stability Operator Of The Helicoid
summary: We consider the Poisson Problem for the stability operator of the helicoid on a vertical strip, under the assumption that the source term is supported on a strip of fixed height. We prove that solutions decay at a definite rate away from the support assuming natural orthogonality conditions on the source term.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the Poisson problem for the helicoid's stability operator on a vertical strip, proving that solutions decay at a definite rate away from the support of the source term under natural orthogonality conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand the decay behavior of solutions to the Poisson problem related to the helicoid's stability operator, which is important for studying the stability and regularity properties of minimal surfaces like helicoids.
AI.method: The paper considers the Poisson problem for the helicoid's stability operator on a vertical strip, assuming the source term is supported on a strip of fixed height, and imposes natural orthogonality conditions on the source term.
AI.result: Solutions decay at a definite rate away from the support region of the source term, given the orthogonality conditions are satisfied.
AI.conclusion: The decay result provides insight into the stability operator's behavior for helicoids, contributing to the broader analysis of minimal surface theory and possibly applications in geometric analysis.

title: Multilevel Adaptive-Rank Methods for Linear and Nonlinear Systems in the Hierarchical Tucker Format
summary: We develop multilevel adaptive-rank iterative methods for the solution of linear and nonlinear systems arising from high-dimensional partial differential equations. Our contributions are threefold. First, we extend the projection method of Ballani and Grasedyck [6] to enable flexible preconditioning of high-dimensional linear systems in low-rank tensor formats. Second, we construct multilevel preconditioning strategies by adapting geometric multigrid methods to the low-rank setting. In contrast to prior work, which primarily employs multigrid as a standalone solver, we emphasize its role as an efficient and robust preconditioner. Third, we integrate these techniques within an inexact Newton framework for the solution of nonlinear systems. The proposed methods are evaluated on a range of model problems, including both linear and nonlinear equations, to assess their convergence behavior and computational efficiency. The results demonstrate that multilevel adaptive-rank strategies yield robust and scalable preconditioners, providing effective solvers for high-dimensional problems in low-rank formats.

AI.tldr: This paper develops multilevel adaptive-rank iterative methods for solving linear and nonlinear systems from high-dimensional PDEs, extending projection methods for flexible preconditioning, adapting geometric multigrid as a preconditioner (not just solver) in low-rank tensor formats, and integrating these into an inexact Newton framework. Results show robust, scalable performance.
AI.motivation: To develop efficient and robust preconditioners and solvers for high-dimensional PDEs in low-rank tensor formats, addressing limitations of prior work that used multigrid mainly as a standalone solver rather than a preconditioner.
AI.method: Three contributions: (1) Extension of Ballani & Grasedyck's projection method for flexible preconditioning of high-dimensional linear systems in low-rank tensor formats; (2) Multilevel preconditioning by adapting geometric multigrid methods to low-rank settings, emphasizing its role as a preconditioner; (3) Integration of these techniques within an inexact Newton framework for nonlinear systems.
AI.result: The multilevel adaptive-rank strategies yield robust and scalable preconditioners, providing effective solvers for high-dimensional problems in low-rank formats, as demonstrated on linear and nonlinear model problems.
AI.conclusion: Multilevel adaptive-rank methods are effective and scalable for solving high-dimensional PDE problems in low-rank tensor formats, serving as robust preconditioners within both linear and nonlinear (inexact Newton) solvers.

title: Linear Tearing Growth and Onset of Relativistic Magnetic Reconnection in the Presence of Shear Flows and a Guide Field
summary: It has been shown in non-relativistic tearing theory that shear flows will slow the linear phase of tearing instability and can delay onset of magnetic reconnection. We find using kinetic particle-in-cell simulations that shear flow as well as guide field strength affect the onset time of relativistic magnetic reconnection. To model this we develop a numerical solver for the growth rate of the relativistic linear tearing instability, including effects of the motional electric field which has not previously been done. We find slowing of growth due to both shear flows and guide field, and at higher flow shear, transition through an intermediate regime to linear Kelvin-Helmholtz instability.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how shear flows and guide fields affect the onset of relativistic magnetic reconnection using kinetic simulations and a new numerical solver for the relativistic linear tearing instability growth rate, finding that both factors slow the instability and can transition to Kelvin-Helmholtz instability at high shear.
AI.motivation: To extend non-relativistic tearing theory findings about shear flow effects into the relativistic regime, where the motional electric field has not been previously considered.
AI.method: Kinetic particle-in-cell (PIC) simulations combined with a novel numerical solver for the relativistic linear tearing instability growth rate that includes motional electric field effects.
AI.result: Shear flows and guide fields both slow the growth of relativistic magnetic reconnection; at high flow shear, the instability transitions through an intermediate regime to linear Kelvin-Helmholtz instability.
AI.conclusion: Shear flows delay relativistic magnetic reconnection onset, similar to non-relativistic theory, with guide field strength also playing a key role, and high shear leads to Kelvin-Helmholtz instability.

title: Boundary-conformal integration for the invariant-imbedding T-matrix method: high-order convergence for faceted particles
comment: 32 pages, 6 figures. Supplementary material included as an ancillary PDF (anc/supplement.pdf). Reference implementation and reproducibility scripts: https://github.com/JuliaRemoteSensing/iitm-convergence (release conformal-v1.0)
summary: The invariant-imbedding T-matrix method (IITM) is a standard tool for light scattering by large, sharply faceted, non-axisymmetric particles (atmospheric ice crystals and mineral dust) where the surface-based extended boundary condition method loses accuracy. Its accuracy is limited by "staircasing": the dielectric contrast of a faceted particle is integrated across boundaries that cut the quadrature grid, so standard quadrature converges at low algebraic order. We show that this non-smoothness has a single geometric origin, the tangencies of the integration sphere to the faces and edges of the particle, which produce jumps, kinks, and half-integer branches according to the tangency type, in all three integration directions. A boundary-conformal scheme removes them using closed-form azimuthal coefficients, panel splitting at the analytically known tangency loci, and a square-root substitution $x \mapsto x_c + t^2$ that absorbs the half-integer branches. For a hexagonal prism the azimuthal integration becomes exact and the zenithal and radial directions recover spectral and fourth-order convergence; because the construction depends only on the contact geometry, it extends to any convex polyhedron, demonstrated on the solid hexagonal bullet (a faceted ice habit with tilted faces). The zenithal crossing is a square-root branch rather than a kink, so the established interval-splitting alone gives only $\mathcal{O}(N^{-3})$, while the radial step removes the half-integer edge branch that caps the Riccati recurrence on faceted particles. The convergence orders are fixed by the local contact geometry and verified size-independent up to $k\,r_{\max} = 20$; what grows with size is the resolution needed to reach each asymptotic regime, not the order.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies the geometric origin of staircasing errors in the invariant-imbedding T-matrix method (IITM) for faceted particles and introduces a boundary-conformal integration scheme that removes these errors, achieving spectral and fourth-order convergence for hexagonal prisms and extensible to any convex polyhedron.
AI.motivation: The IITM suffers from accuracy degradation ("staircasing") when applied to large, sharply faceted, non-axisymmetric particles like atmospheric ice crystals and mineral dust, due to the dielectric contrast being integrated across boundaries that cut the quadrature grid.
AI.method: A boundary-conformal scheme that removes staircasing errors using: (1) closed-form azimuthal coefficients, (2) panel splitting at analytically known tangency loci, and (3) a square-root substitution (x → x_c + t²) to absorb half-integer branches.
AI.result: For hexagonal prisms, azimuthal integration becomes exact, and zenithal/radial directions achieve spectral and fourth-order convergence. Convergence orders are fixed by local contact geometry and verified size-independent up to k·r_max = 20.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method generalizes to any convex polyhedron (demonstrated on a solid hexagonal bullet), and the resolution needed to reach asymptotic regimes grows with size, not the convergence order itself.

title: Finite-Window Recursive Audit Chains for Navier-Stokes Generated Packages
summary: We develop a finite-window recursive audit framework for Navier--Stokes-generated packages. On a fixed window, the underlying anti-phantom certificate asserts that a baseline-visible package is either detected locally or charged to an explicit residual ledger. The first component of the framework is a broad one-step admissibility criterion for pressure-admissible finite-energy Navier--Stokes-generated audit packages under an explicit ledger of synchronization, localization, projection, harmonic-tail, chart, clean-gap, gate/slack, and detector mismatches. The second component is a finite-chain propagation theorem: once one-step admissibility supplies a finite renormalized chain of packages and a static finite-window audit certificate is available at each scale, variable-coefficient error recursion and weighted summation yield a recursive finite-window anti-phantom lower bound. We also give compact/effective pressure projection criteria, reduced quotient chart visibility, clean detector gaps, a pressure--flux--energy matrix kernel condition, a smooth reduced NS-generated verification class, and a conditional Caffarelli--Kohn--Nirenberg compatible defect-extraction criterion.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a finite-window recursive audit framework for Navier-Stokes-generated packages, including admissibility criteria, propagation theorems, and various technical conditions for verifying package legitimacy.
AI.motivation: To create a systematic audit framework that can verify Navier-Stokes-generated packages are either locally detected or properly accounted for in a residual ledger, addressing mismatches and ensuring rigorous verification.
AI.method: The framework consists of a one-step admissibility criterion for pressure-admissible finite-energy packages, a finite-chain propagation theorem using renormalized chains and weighted summation, and compact/effective pressure projection criteria with various technical conditions.
AI.result: A recursive finite-window anti-phantom lower bound is achieved through variable-coefficient error recursion and weighted summation, supported by the developed criteria and conditions.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides rigorous audit mechanisms for Navier-Stokes-generated packages with explicit ledger accountability and detection guarantees.

title: A Moment--Hankel Rank Method for Identifying the Number of Point Sources in the Heat Equation
summary: We develop a low-frequency moment--Hankel rank method for identifying the number of time-independent point sources in the heat equation from boundary flux data. The method is formulated in the unit disk, where the Laplace-transformed and normalized boundary flux admits an explicit Fourier moment representation. By taking the low-frequency limit, we obtain a finite exponential-sum moment sequence in which the nodes encode the source locations, the weights encode the source strengths, and the number of terms equals the number of point sources. The associated Hankel matrix admits a Vandermonde factorization, and its rank is exactly the source number under the natural assumptions that the source locations are distinct and the source strengths are nonzero. We also analyze the effect of discrete and noisy boundary data. A uniform moment perturbation bound is propagated to the empirical Hankel matrix, and Weyl's singular-value perturbation inequality yields a sufficient condition for stable numerical rank recovery in terms of the smallest nonzero singular value of the ideal Hankel matrix. Numerical experiments confirm the exact rank pattern in the noiseless case, validate the stability threshold under moment noise, and illustrate the loss of resolution for close or weak sources. After the source number is identified, the same moment sequence can be used for location and strength recovery through an annihilating-polynomial and Vandermonde reconstruction procedure.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a low-frequency moment–Hankel rank method to identify the number of point sources in the heat equation using boundary flux data, with theoretical stability guarantees and numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To develop a principled, noise-robust method for determining the number of point sources in heat conduction problems from boundary measurements, which is a critical inverse problem parameter.
AI.method: The method uses Laplace-transformed boundary flux in the unit disk to obtain an exponential-sum moment sequence. A Hankel matrix is constructed from these moments, whose rank (via Vandermonde factorization) equals the number of sources under distinct-location and nonzero-strength assumptions. Stability is analyzed using Weyl's inequality and perturbation bounds.
AI.result: Exact rank pattern is observed in noiseless cases; a stability threshold under moment noise is validated; resolution loss for close or weak sources is demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: The moment–Hankel rank method effectively identifies source number, and the same moment sequence can further recover locations and strengths via annihilating-polynomial and Vandermonde reconstruction.

title: Focused Coherent Microwave Scattering for Spatially Resolved Electron Number Density Diagnostics
summary: This work provides an initial demonstration of Focused Coherent Microwave Scattering (F-CMS) diagnostics technique, an extension of the conventional Coherent Microwave Scattering (CMS) technique that enables spatially resolved measurements. The spatial-resolution functionality was achieved using PTFE lenses to focus the probing microwave beam and isolate a distinct probing volume. The feasibility of F-CMS was demonstrated using glow-discharge plasma volume with an electron number density of approximately 5x10$^9$ cm$^{-3}$ as a test object. A clear F-CMS output signal was successfully detected upon the discharge initiation. These findings establish a foundation for the further development of the F-CMS technique, which has the potential to enable highly sensitive, spatiotemporally resolved measurements of electron number density in real time, without the need for signal accumulation (sensitivity down to an electron number density of approximately 10$^8$ cm$^{-3}$ is expected). Such capability will be useful for a variety of applications including diagnostics of unsteady flowfields such as those occurring in hypersonic shock tunnels and spacecraft electric propulsion systems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents Focused Coherent Microwave Scattering (F-CMS), an enhanced diagnostics technique that uses PTFE lenses to focus microwaves for spatially resolved plasma measurements. Tests on glow-discharge plasma (~5×10⁹ cm⁻³) successfully detected scattering signals, with potential sensitivity down to ~10⁸ cm⁻³ for real-time, spatiotemporal electron density measurements.
AI.motivation: To extend conventional Coherent Microwave Scattering (CMS) by adding spatial resolution capability, enabling diagnostics of unsteady flowfields in hypersonic shock tunnels and spacecraft electric propulsion systems where existing techniques lack spatiotemporal resolution.
AI.method: PTFE lenses were used to focus the probing microwave beam and isolate a distinct probing volume, achieving spatially resolved measurements via Focused Coherent Microwave Scattering (F-CMS).
AI.result: Clear F-CMS output signals were successfully detected from glow-discharge plasma with electron density ~5×10⁹ cm⁻³ upon discharge initiation, demonstrating feasibility.
AI.conclusion: F-CMS shows potential for highly sensitive, real-time spatiotemporal electron density measurements without signal accumulation, with expected sensitivity down to ~10⁸ cm⁻³, useful for unsteady flow diagnostics.

title: Elastic Non-Uniform FFT (ENUFFT) spectral reconstruction of irregularly sampled orography on unstructured grids
comment: 48 pages, 7 figures, 10 tables. Submitted to JAMES
summary: Subgrid-scale orography remains a leading source of uncertainty in numerical modeling because terrain spectra must be recovered from irregularly sampled elevation data and then reduced to a flow-dependent launch budget for parameterizations. Existing approaches are limited either by assuming regular samples on rectangular grids or by fitting coefficients whose truncation and regularization effects become embedded in the spectrum. None achieves dynamic, flow-dependent truncation. This study introduces an Elastic Non-Uniform Fast Fourier Transform (ENUFFT) framework that computes local Fourier coefficients directly from irregularly sampled orography on unstructured grids, without interpolation or fitting. It combines a type-1 NUFFT with local windowing, quadrature weights, and a new Elastic Mode Selection (EMS) algorithm for retaining a local flow-dependent subset of modes. ENUFFT is compared with the strongest relevant existing method in a monochromatic and a real Alpine terrain test. In both cases, it recovers peak amplitude and direction comparably while significantly compacting the spectra (monochromatic ~25%, Alpine ~60%). It also satisfies the Parseval condition more closely with its spectral variance (energy) deviating from reference by ~14-24% versus ~500-122,000% for the existing method. Its EMS is additionally tested in a mountain-wave test where it reduces the launch spectrum by >=75% while keeping launch-power loss <=7%. Along with better compute scaling, ENUFFT is thus a computationally efficient, physically interpretable framework that can make Fourier-based orographic source descriptions practical for spectral-budget-aware parameterizations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ENUFFT, a framework for computing Fourier coefficients from irregularly sampled orography on unstructured grids, which significantly improves spectral compactness, energy conservation, and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Subgrid-scale orography is a major uncertainty source in numerical modeling because terrain spectra must be recovered from irregular samples and reduced to a flow-dependent launch budget. Existing approaches are limited by assuming regular grids or embedding truncation/regularization effects, and none achieve dynamic flow-dependent truncation.
AI.method: The ENUFFT framework combines a type-1 Non-Uniform Fast Fourier Transform (NUFFT) with local windowing, quadrature weights, and a new Elastic Mode Selection (EMS) algorithm that retains a local flow-dependent subset of Fourier modes directly from irregularly sampled orography on unstructured grids, without interpolation or fitting.
AI.result: ENUFFT recovers peak amplitude and direction comparably to existing methods while compacting spectra significantly (monochromatic ~25%, Alpine ~60%). It satisfies the Parseval condition much better (spectral variance deviation ~14-24% vs ~500-122,000% for existing methods). EMS reduces launch spectrum by ≥75% while keeping launch-power loss ≤7%.
AI.conclusion: ENUFFT is a computationally efficient, physically interpretable framework that makes Fourier-based orographic source descriptions practical for spectral-budget-aware parameterizations in numerical weather and climate modeling.

title: Locally period homogenization of multiscale model for plant tissue biomechanics
summary: In this work, the derivation and multiscale analysis of a mathematical model for plant tissue biomechanics is considered. The microscopic model consists of a coupled systems of equations of linear elasticity, describing mechanical deformation, and reaction-diffusion and ordinary differential equations modelling the dynamics of load-bearing cross-links in cell walls and middle lamella, connecting individual cells in a plant tissue. It takes into account a two-way coupling between mechanical deformation and chemical processes in cell walls and middle lamella, where elastic properties depend on the density of cross-links and reactions terms, defining the stretching and breakage of the cross-links, depend on the deformation gradient. The nonlinear dependence of solutions of the ordinary differential equation on the displacement gradient and of the boundary conditions in reaction-diffusion problem on the displacement induces novel approaches in the derivation of a priori estimates. Using homogenization techniques of locally periodic (l-p) two-scale convergence, a macroscopic model for plant tissue biomechanics is derived, representing the first result in the multiscale analysis of a biomechanical model for tissues with non-periodic fibrous microstructure. For multiscale analysis and derivation of the macroscopic model, the non-periodic microstructure of plant cell walls, characterised by rotated planes of parallel aligned microfibrils, is approximated by the corresponding locally periodic microstructure. The two-way coupling between equations of linear elasticity and reaction-diffusion and ordinary differential equations requires the proof of the strong l-p two-scale convergence for the deformation gradient and for the density of stretched cross-links, in order to pass to the limit in the nonlinear functions.

AI.tldr: This paper derives and analyzes a multiscale mathematical model for plant tissue biomechanics, incorporating two-way coupling between mechanical deformation and chemical cross-link dynamics, and obtains a macroscopic model via locally periodic two-scale homogenization techniques.
AI.motivation: To develop a rigorous multiscale framework for plant tissue biomechanics that accounts for non-periodic fibrous microstructure and two-way coupling between elasticity and chemical processes in cell walls and middle lamella.
AI.method: The authors use locally periodic (l-p) two-scale convergence homogenization techniques, approximating non-periodic fibrous microstructures with locally periodic ones. They prove strong l-p two-scale convergence for deformation gradient and stretched cross-link density to handle nonlinear coupling.
AI.result: A macroscopic model for plant tissue biomechanics is derived, representing the first multiscale analysis result for a biomechanical model with non-periodic fibrous microstructure.
AI.conclusion: The work successfully bridges micro- and macroscale descriptions of plant tissue mechanics, providing a foundation for understanding how microscopic cross-link dynamics in cell walls influence macroscopic tissue behavior.

title: Spectrally Safe Neural Operator Warm-Starts for Large-Scale Newton Solvers
summary: Neural operators are increasingly used to warm-start Newton solvers for nonlinear PDEs, on the premise that a low test error places the initial guess inside the basin of attraction. We show that this premise is unreliable. An operator trained to the relative \(L^2\) error \(O(10^{-3})\) can still produce an initial state in which the discrete Jacobian is indefinite, because the mean-squared training controls error on average while leaving localized pointwise violations of the underlying physics. For a nearly incompressible hyperelasticity problem, we trace this to the predicted volume change: the operator disperses \(\mathrm{det} F\) well away from one, and the resulting Jacobian acquires negative eigenvalues even when the predicted field is visually indistinguishable from the reference. At a small scale, this is a nuisance; at a multi-million degree-of-freedom scale, it is disqualifying, since the conjugate gradient and other Krylov solvers needed for memory-feasible Newton steps assume a definite spectrum. We then show that a short, label-free fine-tuning phase -- penalizing the operator against the discrete energy, with no additional solution data -- shifts the Jacobian spectrum back to positive definite. Combined with an inexact outer loop, this gives a warm-started Newton method that converges across the full loading range where the unregularized operator fails, reaching up to 5.4\(\times\) wall-clock speedup over incremental continuation on a 3D problem with 6.4 million degrees of freedom.

AI.tldr: Neural operators can produce low L2 error initial guesses for Newton solvers but still yield indefinite Jacobians due to localized physics violations; a label-free fine-tuning phase penalizing discrete energy shifts the spectrum to positive definite, enabling convergent warm-started Newton with up to 5.4x speedup on 6.4M DOF problems.
AI.motivation: To challenge the common premise that low test error from neural operators reliably places initial guesses inside the basin of attraction for Newton solvers, and to propose a fix.
AI.method: Fine-tuning the neural operator by penalizing against the discrete energy (without additional solution data) to shift the Jacobian spectrum to positive definite, combined with an inexact outer loop.
AI.result: The unregularized operator with O(10^{-3}) relative L2 error can produce indefinite Jacobians; the proposed fine-tuning restores positive definiteness and enables convergence across the full loading range, achieving up to 5.4x wall-clock speedup over incremental continuation on a 6.4M DOF 3D problem.
AI.conclusion: Low L2 error alone is insufficient for reliable warm-starting of Newton solvers; energy-based fine-tuning is an effective remedy.

title: Plasma Flow Generation and Particle Acceleration from Expanding Magnetic Bubbles
summary: Impulsive plasma dynamics in the laboratory are often driven by rising electric currents, yet their quantitative plasma response has not been well established. By means of fully kinetic particle-in-cell simulations and laser-driven capacitor-coil experiments, we show that a rising current expels plasma, forming an expanding magnetic bubble and accelerating particles. The expansion front velocity scales with the Alfvén speed determined by the magnetic field at its inner edge and the plasma density at its outer edge. This mechanism establishes impulsive current drive as a fundamental way that generates plasma flows and accelerates particles in laboratory plasmas, with potential relevance to astrophysics.

AI.tldr: This paper uses simulations and experiments to show that a rising electric current expels plasma, forming an expanding magnetic bubble and accelerating particles, with expansion velocity scaling with the Alfvén speed.
AI.motivation: The quantitative plasma response to rising electric currents in impulsive plasma dynamics has not been well established.
AI.method: Fully kinetic particle-in-cell simulations and laser-driven capacitor-coil experiments.
AI.result: A rising current expels plasma, forming an expanding magnetic bubble and accelerating particles. The expansion front velocity scales with the Alfvén speed determined by the magnetic field at its inner edge and the plasma density at its outer edge.
AI.conclusion: Impulsive current drive is a fundamental mechanism that generates plasma flows and accelerates particles in laboratory plasmas, with potential relevance to astrophysics.

title: A dressed polarizability framework for interface-coupled meta-atoms and large-scale metasurfaces
summary: The modeling of large collections of interacting meta-atoms remains a central challenge in photonics because it requires the simultaneous treatment of complex scatterer geometries, multiple scattering, and heterogeneous environments. Here, we introduce the dressed Global Polarizability Matrix (dGPM), a reduced-order electromagnetic framework in which complex meta-atoms are represented by compact scattering operators that explicitly incorporate the influence of nearby interfaces. The dGPM accurately reconstructs near- and far-field electromagnetic responses for interface-coupled structures, including geometries beyond the practical applicability of conventional T-matrix approaches. The framework naturally combines interface-coupled and free-space scatterers within a unified multiple-scattering formalism and enables simulations of large ordered and disordered metasurfaces. More broadly, the dGPM extends operator-based electromagnetic modeling to heterogeneous photonic environments while preserving a compact scatterer-based representation, providing an efficient framework for the simulation and design of large-scale photonic systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces dressed Global Polarizability Matrix (dGPM), a reduced-order electromagnetic framework that represents complex meta-atoms as compact scattering operators incorporating nearby interface effects, enabling efficient simulation of large ordered and disordered metasurfaces in heterogeneous photonic environments.
AI.motivation: Modeling large collections of interacting meta-atoms is a central challenge in photonics due to the need to simultaneously handle complex scatterer geometries, multiple scattering, and heterogeneous environments.
AI.method: The dGPM framework represents complex meta-atoms using compact scattering operators that explicitly incorporate the influence of nearby interfaces, combining interface-coupled and free-space scatterers within a unified multiple-scattering formalism.
AI.result: dGPM accurately reconstructs near- and far-field electromagnetic responses for interface-coupled structures, including geometries beyond conventional T-matrix approaches, and enables simulations of large ordered and disordered metasurfaces.
AI.conclusion: dGPM extends operator-based electromagnetic modeling to heterogeneous photonic environments while preserving a compact scatterer-based representation, providing an efficient framework for simulating and designing large-scale photonic systems.

title: Non-uniqueness for the hypo-dissipative compressible 3D magnetohydrodynamic equations
summary: We consider the compressible 3D magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) equations under general pressure laws. For all hypo-viscosities $(-Δ)^{α_1}$ and hypo-resistivity $(-Δ)^{α_2}$ with $α_1,α_2\in(0,1)$, we prove the non-uniqueness of weak solutions to 3D MHD equations which reveals that there exist infinitely many weak solutions with the same initial data. Also, for the weak solutions in $C^{\widetildeβ}_{t,x}$ to the compressible ideal MHD, where $\widetildeβ>0$, we prove that they are the strong vanishing viscosity and resistivity limit of the weak solutions to the hypo-dissipative compressible MHD.

AI.tldr: This paper proves non-uniqueness of weak solutions to compressible 3D MHD equations with hypo-viscosity and hypo-resistivity, and shows that sufficiently regular weak solutions of ideal MHD are vanishing dissipation limits of hypo-dissipative solutions.
AI.motivation: To understand the uniqueness versus non-uniqueness of weak solutions to compressible MHD equations under fractional dissipation, and to connect ideal MHD solutions with dissipative approximations.
AI.method: Uses convex integration techniques to construct infinitely many weak solutions for hypo-viscous/hypo-resistive MHD, and analyzes the vanishing dissipation limit for Hölder regular solutions of ideal MHD.
AI.result: Non-uniqueness holds for all hypo-viscosity and hypo-resistivity parameters in (0,1); weak solutions of ideal MHD with positive Hölder regularity are strong limits of hypo-dissipative solutions.
AI.conclusion: Fractional dissipation alone is insufficient to guarantee uniqueness for compressible MHD, but higher regularity of ideal solutions ensures they can be approximated by dissipative ones.

title: A projected walk on spheres method for elliptic equations on high-dimensional embedded manifolds
summary: In this paper, we propose a projected Walk on Spheres method (PWoS) for screened Poisson equations on embedded manifolds. The method employs local extensions together with the Green representation in local Euclidean balls, coupled with a closest-point projection that maps the boundary samples back to the manifold. This formulation yields a meshfree and highly parallelizable stochastic recursion in the ambient Euclidean space, rather than a direct discretization of the Laplace-Beltrami operator on the manifold. To recover the intrinsic geometric structure of the problem, we introduce a compensation term for the discrepancy between the ambient Laplacian of the closest-point extension and the Laplace-Beltrami operator on the manifold, together with an adaptive radius strategy determined by local geometric and boundary information. Under the geometric projection and prescribed compensation accuracy, we establish mean-square error estimates for the proposed Monte Carlo method in both the boundary and closed-manifold settings. Extensive numerical examples on parametrized, implicit, high-dimensional (up to 1000 dimensions), and point-cloud manifolds are presented to illustrate the convergence and efficiency of the proposed method across different geometries.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Projected Walk on Spheres (PWoS) method for solving screened Poisson equations on embedded manifolds using local extensions, Green's representation, closest-point projection, and a compensation term, achieving meshfree, parallelizable Monte Carlo sampling with proven mean-square error estimates.
AI.motivation: To solve screened Poisson equations on embedded manifolds without directly discretizing the Laplace-Beltrami operator, enabling a meshfree and parallelizable approach that handles complex geometries including high-dimensional and point-cloud manifolds.
AI.method: The method combines local extensions with Green's representation in local Euclidean balls, uses closest-point projection to map boundary samples back to the manifold, introduces a compensation term for the discrepancy between ambient Laplacian and Laplace-Beltrami operator, and employs an adaptive radius strategy based on local geometric and boundary information.
AI.result: Mean-square error estimates are established for both boundary and closed-manifold settings. Numerical examples on parametrized, implicit, high-dimensional (up to 1000 dimensions), and point-cloud manifolds demonstrate convergence and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The PWoS method provides an effective, meshfree, and parallelizable stochastic approach for screened Poisson equations on manifolds, with theoretical guarantees and broad applicability across diverse geometric representations.

title: Plasma turbulence driven by wave-hole interaction
summary: Wave-obstacle diffraction is, par excellence, an example of transition to nonlinearity, generating turbulence and complexity in fluids. We present an idealized kinetic plasma regime capturing this ubiquitous interaction and its transition to phase-space turbulence. High-resolution Vlasov-Poisson simulations reveal that the interplay between electrostatic waves and density gaps at Debye and sub-Debye scales redistributes a strongly anisotropic energy cascade throughout the full phase space, unveiling the effect of inhomogeneities on structure formation and small-scale-directed turbulent flow.

AI.tldr: The paper uses high-resolution Vlasov-Poisson simulations to study wave-obstacle diffraction in plasma, revealing how electrostatic wave interactions with density gaps at Debye scales drive anisotropic energy cascades and phase-space turbulence.
AI.motivation: To understand how wave-obstacle diffraction transitions to nonlinearity and generates turbulence/complexity in plasma systems, particularly focusing on phase-space turbulence.
AI.method: High-resolution Vlasov-Poisson simulations examining electrostatic wave interactions with density gaps at Debye and sub-Debye scales.
AI.result: The interplay between waves and density gaps redistributes strongly anisotropic energy cascades throughout full phase space, revealing inhomogeneity effects on structure formation and small-scale turbulent flow.
AI.conclusion: Wave-obstacle diffraction in kinetic plasma drives phase-space turbulence through anisotropic energy cascades at small scales, with inhomogeneities playing a key role.

title: Bulk Photovoltaic Effect in Two-Dimensional Perovskite Oxides
comment: 10 figures and 22 pages are included in the main text and supplementary material. Accepted for publication in Physical Review B as a Regular Article
summary: Perovskite oxides ABO$_3$ host a rich interplay of charge, spin, lattice, and orbital degrees of freedom, giving rise to diverse quantum phenomena. In low-dimensional ABO$_3$, reduced symmetry can induce exotic quantum effects such as the two-dimensional electron gas and unconventional superconductivity. Using first-principles density functional theory, tight-binding modeling, and symmetry analysis, we show that ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) ABO$_3$ films -- exemplified by SrTiO$_3$ -- naturally break inversion symmetry, producing a spontaneous out-of-plane bulk photovoltaic (BPV) effect. This differs from previous studies on in-plane BPV current signals and is more applicable and experimentally detectable. Such an effect is highly tunable via thickness, strain, surface termination, crystallographic orientation, and Moiré twisting. These findings are broadly applicable to a wide range of 2D perovskite and other layer-resolved oxides.

AI.tldr: Ultrathin 2D perovskite oxide films (e.g., SrTiO₃) naturally break inversion symmetry, producing a spontaneous out-of-plane bulk photovoltaic effect that is tunable via thickness, strain, surface termination, orientation, and Moiré twisting.
AI.motivation: To explore how reduced symmetry in low-dimensional perovskite oxides can induce exotic quantum effects, specifically the out-of-plane bulk photovoltaic effect, which is more applicable and experimentally detectable than previously studied in-plane BPV signals.
AI.method: First-principles density functional theory (DFT), tight-binding modeling, and symmetry analysis were used to study ultrathin 2D ABO₃ films (e.g., SrTiO₃).
AI.result: Ultrathin 2D ABO₃ films break inversion symmetry, generating a spontaneous out-of-plane BPV effect. This effect is highly tunable via thickness, strain, surface termination, crystallographic orientation, and Moiré twisting.
AI.conclusion: The findings are broadly applicable to a wide range of 2D perovskite and other layer-resolved oxides, offering a new route to exploit the bulk photovoltaic effect in low-dimensional systems.

title: On $m$-order logarithmic Schrödinger operator
