AI.tldr: Researchers improved a numerical method (MCCNIM) for solving the nonlinear Burgers' equation by reformulating the convection term to use previous time-level values, making the computation cheaper while keeping accuracy. The new method (RCCNIM) is tested and shows similar accuracy but faster performance.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational cost of the original MCCNIM method for solving the nonlinear Burgers' equation while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: RCCNIM reformulates the nonlinear convection term by evaluating convective velocity using cell-averaged values from the previous time level instead of present-time approximation, resulting in a fully algebraic system with coefficients depending only on known quantities.
AI.result: RCCNIM achieves comparable accuracy to MCCNIM with significantly reduced computational cost for the nonlinear Burgers' equation.
AI.conclusion: RCCNIM is a more efficient alternative to MCCNIM, with potential for extension to more complex nonlinear fluid flow problems.

title: Disambiguation of magnetic sensors in ITER
summary: ITER will possess approximately 500 magnetic sensors (mainly measuring poloidal flux) distributed across the first wall. The coils are at known locations but the matching signals not necessarily known. There may also be mistakes in the wiring of the coil polarity. The existing strategy to disambiguate coils uses combinatoric programming of poloidal field coil waveforms of up to 48 discharges of plasma-less operation. An alternate strategy explored in this work is the energisation of a combination of both poloidal and toroidally asymmetric active coils, and Biot-Savart computation of the field solution from all active coils at the sensor coils. A direct brute force permutation of all $N$ coil combinations scales as $O(2^N N!) $ which is intractable for $N>10$. The mathematically formulated optimisation problem was analysed using AI-assisted coding tools, which identified the problem structure as a signed assignment problem and suggested a Hungarian-algorithm-based optimisation strategy, which scales as $O(N^3)$. This search algorithm, when embedded into the magnetic-diagnostic identification problem, was able to disambiguate randomly connected and polarised coils in a regularly spaced array in the ITER first wall (where the the coils are located) down to a signal-to-noise ratio of 50. The computation took 2 seconds. Reconstruction of the actual coil positions in the ITER first wall was achieved high confidence, $C>0.97$. Reconstruction of the second wall poloidal flux coils, which comprised multiple arrays at near constant $φ$ (each of which is regularly spaced in $θ$) had a much lower confidence, of $C > 0.15$. By adding the active poloidal field coils to the combined cost function, the confidence increased to $C > 0.59$. This provides the opportunity to reduce the commissioning time of ITER, and is a strategy that could be tested on other toroidal magnetic confinement devices.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a Hungarian-algorithm-based optimization strategy (scaling O(N³) instead of O(2^N N!)) to disambiguate randomly connected and polarized magnetic sensor coils in ITER's first wall, achieving high-confidence reconstruction (C>0.97) for first-wall coils and improved confidence (C>0.59) for second-wall poloidal flux coils by including active poloidal field coils in the cost function.
AI.motivation: ITER's ~500 magnetic sensors distributed across the first wall have known locations but unknown matching signals and potential wiring polarity errors. The existing combinatorial strategy using up to 48 plasma-less discharges is complex and time-consuming, motivating the need for a more efficient disambiguation method.
AI.method: The problem was formulated as a signed assignment problem and solved using a Hungarian-algorithm-based optimization strategy (O(N³) complexity). Active poloidal and toroidally asymmetric coils are energized, and Biot-Savart computations provide the field solution at sensor locations. The algorithm searches for optimal matching between measured and expected signals.
AI.result: For regularly spaced first-wall coils: signal-to-noise ratio down to 50, computation time 2 seconds, reconstruction confidence C>0.97. For second-wall poloidal flux coils (multiple arrays at constant φ): confidence C>0.15 alone, improved to C>0.59 by adding active poloidal field coils to the cost function.
AI.conclusion: The Hungarian-algorithm approach efficiently solves the coil disambiguation problem, offering potential to reduce ITER's commissioning time. The strategy is also applicable to other toroidal magnetic confinement devices.

title: An interpretable closed form for entanglement entropy from bitstrings, guided by a graph neural network
summary: The empirical bitstring distribution is the most accessible observable on Rydberg-atom arrays, but the bipartite von~Neumann entropy it constrains is far costlier to obtain. We present a six-term linear closed form for the entropy, built on bitstring-derivable physics scalars, and characterize its accuracy, portability, scaling behaviour, and calibration cost. The feature set is selected with guidance from a trained graph neural network: probing the network localizes its entropy prediction to the two-point correlators on the bipartition boundary, and an exhaustive ground-truth search restricted to those boundary correlators isolates the form. It reaches $0.024$~nats mean absolute error in distribution: $6.4$ times the network's error, but in a form a human can read and apply without retraining. Fit once and applied unchanged, it has lower error than the base network on five of six out-of-distribution pools and ties the sixth. An independent density-matrix renormalization-group study to one hundred atoms -- five times the reach of exact diagonalization -- settles the size-extrapolation question: coefficients frozen at small size fail at scale, but the failure is structured. Refit per size the form holds to $25$--$50$~mnat (cross-validated); two of its six slopes follow clean inverse-size laws, one a downward curving growth, and the others are trendless; the fitted laws deploy the form label-free at roughly $40$--$80$~mnat. The result fixes a label-budget rule: at large sizes, a few dozen labels recalibrate the closed form to match a fine-tuned in-distribution ensemble on the same features, while nonlinear ML models pull ahead only given large labelled datasets.

AI.tldr: A six-term linear closed form for bipartite von Neumann entropy is derived from bitstring distributions on Rydberg-atom arrays. It achieves 0.024 nats mean absolute error, generalizes to out-of-distribution data, and scales to 100 atoms via DMRG. Refitting per size fixes scaling failures, enabling label-free deployment with ~40-80 mnat error.
AI.motivation: To derive an interpretable, portable, and retraining-free closed-form expression for bipartite von Neumann entropy from easily accessible bitstring distributions on Rydberg-atom arrays, overcoming the high cost of direct entropy measurements.
AI.method: A graph neural network (GNN) is trained to predict entropy; probing localizes its prediction to two-point correlators on the bipartition boundary. An exhaustive search over these boundary correlators yields a six-term linear closed form. DMRG extrapolates to 100 atoms to study size scaling.
AI.result: The closed form achieves 0.024 nats mean absolute error (6.4× the GNN but interpretable). It outperforms the base GNN on 5/6 out-of-distribution pools. Coefficients frozen at small size fail at scale, but refitting per size yields 25-50 mnat error. Two coefficients follow clean inverse-size laws; fitted laws enable label-free deployment at ~40-80 mnat.
AI.conclusion: An interpretable closed-form entropy estimator from bitstrings is viable. A label-budget rule is established: few dozen labels suffice to recalibrate the form at large sizes, while nonlinear ML models only pull ahead with large labeled datasets.

title: Finite-dimensional reduction of a Wasserstein gradient flow and sharp decay rates
summary: We study the Wasserstein gradient flow generated by a family of extended generalized variance functionals, defined as the expected squared $n$-dimensional volume of a simplex, which includes the classical variance-type interaction and generalized variance as special cases. The key structural observation is that this functional depends only on the covariance matrix. Consequently, the Wasserstein gradient flow reduces to a finite-dimensional system for the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix, and the full measure-valued solution can be recovered through an explicit linear pushforward representation. Using this representation, we establish global well-posedness for arbitrary initial data in $\mathcal P_2(\mathbb R^d)$ without assuming compact support. We also study the long-time behavior of the flow. For every initial datum in $\mathcal P_2(\mathbb R^d)$, the solution converges to a limiting equilibrium measure whose covariance has rank strictly less than $n$. Moreover, we obtain sharp convergence rates in all spectral regimes: exponential in the non-degenerate case and algebraic with the optimal exponent in the degenerate case.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Wasserstein gradient flows generated by extended generalized variance functionals, showing the dynamics reduce to a finite-dimensional eigenvalue system for the covariance matrix. The authors establish global well-posedness, characterize convergence to lower-rank equilibrium measures, and prove sharp convergence rates (exponential or optimal algebraic) depending on spectral degeneracy.
AI.motivation: To understand the Wasserstein gradient flow structure of generalized variance functionals that depend only on the covariance matrix, and to rigorously analyze global well-posedness, long-time behavior, and sharp convergence rates across different spectral regimes.
AI.method: The authors exploit the key structural fact that the functional depends solely on the covariance matrix, reducing the infinite-dimensional Wasserstein gradient flow to a finite-dimensional ODE system for the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix. The full measure-valued solution is recovered via an explicit linear pushforward representation.
AI.result: Global well-posedness for arbitrary initial data in P₂(Rᵈ) without compact support; convergence to a limiting equilibrium measure whose covariance has rank strictly less than n; sharp convergence rates (exponential in non-degenerate case, algebraic with optimal exponent in degenerate case).
AI.conclusion: The extended generalized variance functionals generate well-behaved Wasserstein gradient flows with complete convergence analysis, and the reduction to finite-dimensional eigenvalue dynamics offers a powerful analytical framework for covariance-dependent functionals.

title: Counting Triangles of Graphs via Randomized Trace Estimation with Incomplete Matrix-Vector Products
summary: Counting triangles in graphs is a fundamental operation in network analysis, underpinning metrics such as clustering coefficients and serving as a signal for community detection, link prediction, and anomaly detection. The standard approach computes the trace of the cube of the adjacency matrix, but explicitly forming $\mathbf{A}^3$ is infeasible for large graphs. Hutchinson randomized trace estimator offers an efficient alternative by approximating the trace through stochastic averaging of quadratic forms, requiring only matrix vector products with $\mathbf{A}$. However, in distributed and heterogeneous computing environments, observing all entries of these products can be costly due to communication overhead and straggler effects. To address this, we propose a new variant of Hutchinson estimator that operates under partial observation constraints, where both the number and identities of observed entries are random. We provide theoretical guarantees on unbiasedness, variance bounds, and sample complexity, and demonstrate through experiments on synthetic and real world graphs that our method achieves accurate triangle count estimates while reducing synchronization costs. This work highlights the adaptability of randomized algorithms to modern computational architectures and opens avenues for efficient motif counting in large scale network analytics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a variant of Hutchinson's randomized trace estimator that works under partial observation constraints to efficiently count triangles in large graphs, reducing communication overhead in distributed environments while providing theoretical guarantees on unbiasedness and variance.
AI.motivation: Standard triangle counting via computing the trace of A³ is infeasible for large graphs. Hutchinson's estimator requires full observation of matrix-vector products, which is costly in distributed/heterogeneous environments due to communication overhead and straggler effects.
AI.method: A new variant of Hutchinson's randomized trace estimator that operates under partial observation constraints, where both the number and identities of observed entries are random.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees on unbiasedness, variance bounds, and sample complexity are provided. Experiments on synthetic and real-world graphs demonstrate accurate triangle count estimates with reduced synchronization costs.
AI.conclusion: The work shows randomized algorithms can adapt to modern computational architectures, opening avenues for efficient motif counting in large-scale network analytics.

title: Detachment dynamics and disturbance rejection in the TCV X-Point Target divertor
summary: The X-Point Target divertor is an alternative divertor configuration with a secondary X-point in its divertor volume. In this work, we investigate the dynamic response and disturbance rejection capacity of the XPT configuration on the TCV tokamak, comparing it to a single null (SN) divertor. We employ a system identification approach using multi-sine perturbations to measure the dynamic response of the detached state in both Ohmic and auxiliary-heated L-mode scenarios upon D$_2$ fuelling, N$_2$ seeding and Electron Resonance Cyclotron Heating (ECRH) power modulations. We demonstrate an inherent disturbance rejection capacity of the XPT at its secondary X-point compared to a SN configuration for all perturbation scenarios. Upstream of its secondary X-point, the dynamic response of the detached state between the XPT and SN appears similar. The disturbance rejection capacity of the XPT could be highly beneficial for passively buffering disturbances that cannot be effectively managed by power exhaust controllers. At the same time, it presents a challenge for monitoring the detached state close to the secondary x-point.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the dynamic response and disturbance rejection capacity of the X-Point Target (XPT) divertor configuration on the TCV tokamak, comparing it to a standard single null (SN) divertor. Using system identification with multi-sine perturbations, the authors show that XPT inherently rejects disturbances near its secondary X-point better than SN across various scenarios, though upstream responses remain similar.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether the X-Point Target divertor configuration offers better dynamic disturbance rejection compared to a conventional single null divertor, which could be beneficial for managing power exhaust in fusion devices where controllers may be insufficient.
AI.method: System identification using multi-sine perturbations to measure dynamic response of the detached state under D₂ fuelling, N₂ seeding, and ECRH power modulations in Ohmic and L-mode scenarios on TCV.
AI.result: The XPT configuration demonstrates inherent disturbance rejection capacity at its secondary X-point compared to SN across all perturbation scenarios, while upstream responses remain similar between the two configurations.
AI.conclusion: The XPT's disturbance rejection capacity is beneficial for passively buffering disturbances beyond controller reach, but poses challenges for monitoring the detached state near the secondary X-point.

title: Development, Validation, and Benchmarking of a Multidisciplinary Semi-Analytical Model for Wave Energy Converters
comment: 75 pages, 45 figures. To be submitted to Applied Ocean Research journal. See code at https://github.com/symbiotic-engineering/MDOcean/tree/main and reproducibility package at https://calkit.io/symbiotic-engineering/mdocean
summary:
Wave energy converters (WECs) require system-level techno-economic analysis to balance power production, cost, and survivability. Existing simulation tools are either too computationally costly for large-scale optimization or too narrow in disciplinary scope to support integrated design studies. This work presents MDOcean, a novel open-source WEC simulation framework for rapid early-stage design exploration, parametric analysis, and multidisciplinary optimization. MDOcean integrates hydrodynamics, dynamics, structures, and economics in a computationally efficient architecture based on analytical and semi-analytical methods that substantially reduce runtime while maintaining near-numerical accuracy.
  The framework includes an eigenfunction-based linear hydrodynamic solver, a quasi-linearized frequency-domain dynamics engine capable of modeling drag and saturation nonlinearities, a structural sizing module incorporating realistic yield, ultimate, buckling, storm, and fatigue design criteria, and a simple cost model for techno-economic assessment. Particular emphasis is placed on the linearized pseudo-spectral optimal control formulation, which extends frequency-domain constraint-handling approaches with a unified describing-function and analytical quadratically-constrained quadratic program framework. This formulation efficiently treats nonlinearities and constraints while preserving compatibility with optimization and frequency-domain analysis techniques.
  Validation and benchmarking demonstrate that MDOcean's 151 ms runtime is orders of magnitude faster than leading WEC simulation tools while maintaining agreement with higher-fidelity baselines to within a few percent in most cases. The framework also provides insight into limiting behaviors, scaling laws, subsystem interactions, and key tradeoffs governing WEC design and techno-economic performance.

AI.tldr: MDOcean is a fast open-source WEC simulation framework integrating hydrodynamics, dynamics, structures, and economics for rapid early-stage design exploration, achieving 151 ms runtime with near-numerical accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing WEC simulation tools are either too computationally costly for large-scale optimization or too narrow in disciplinary scope to support integrated design studies. System-level techno-economic analysis balancing power, cost, and survivability is needed.
AI.method: MDOcean integrates eigenfunction-based linear hydrodynamics, quasi-linearized frequency-domain dynamics (with drag/saturation nonlinearities), structural sizing (yield, fatigue, storm, buckling), cost modeling, and a linearized pseudo-spectral optimal control formulation using describing functions and QCQP.
AI.result: 151 ms runtime — orders of magnitude faster than leading WEC tools — while maintaining agreement with higher-fidelity baselines to within a few percent in most cases.
AI.conclusion: MDOcean enables rapid early-stage design exploration, parametric analysis, and multidisciplinary optimization for WECs, providing insight into limiting behaviors, scaling laws, subsystem interactions, and key tradeoffs.

title: Absence of eigenvalues of dissipative operator for strictly convex obstacles
summary: We study the wave equation in the exterior of a strictly convex bounded domain $K \subset {\mathbb R}^d, d \geq 3,$ odd, with dissipative boundary condition $\partial_ν u - γ(x) \partial_t u = 0$ on the boundary $Γ$ and $0 < γ(x) <1, \:\forall x \in Γ.$ The solutions are described by a contraction semigroup $V(t) = e^{tG}, \: t \geq 0.$ In [10] we established that for $γ\equiv const$ and $K = \{x \in {\mathbb R}^3: \:|x| \leq 1\}$ the operator $G$ has no eigenvalues and we conjectured that the same result holds for every strictly convex obstacle. In this paper we prove this conjecture.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that the generator of the contraction semigroup for the wave equation with dissipative boundary conditions outside a strictly convex obstacle has no eigenvalues, confirming a previous conjecture.
AI.motivation: To prove the conjecture from prior work [10] that for any strictly convex obstacle (not just a sphere), the operator G has no eigenvalues under dissipative boundary conditions.
AI.method: The authors study the wave equation with dissipative boundary condition ∂_ν u - γ(x) ∂_t u = 0 where 0<γ(x)<1, using a contraction semigroup V(t)=e^{tG} and extend results from the constant γ and spherical case to general strictly convex domains.
AI.result: The conjecture is proven: for every strictly convex obstacle K ⊂ ℝ^d (d≥3 odd) with dissipative boundary condition, the operator G has no eigenvalues.
AI.conclusion: The absence of eigenvalues for the generator G is established for all strictly convex obstacles, generalizing the special case of a sphere with constant γ.

title: Interpolations: a linear algebra approach
summary:
This paper presents a unified linear-algebraic framework for interpolation problems, viewing different standart problems (Lagrange polynomials, Hermite splines, and trigonometric interpolation) with the help of dual spaces. By representing interpolation conditions as linear functionals in the dual of a finite-dimensional function space, we establish a general existence and uniqueness theorem.
  We derive explicit formulas for basis functions dual to given interpolation schemes, enabling efficient construction of interpolants as linear combinations of these bases.
  The approach is extended to trigonometric interpolation, where we provide closed-form expressions for dual bases and analyze degeneracy conditions. We also address practical scenarios involving heterogeneous sensors, where function values and derivatives are specified at distinct points, and propose a corresponding interpolation scheme. The method is systematic, dimension-aware, and decouples the analysis of interpolation conditions from the choice of basis functions.
  We cosider examples from kinematics and periodic function interpolation demonstrate the versatility of the approach, suggesting applications in trajectory planning, signal processing, and beyond.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a unified linear-algebraic framework for interpolation problems using dual spaces, establishing general existence/uniqueness theorems, deriving explicit dual basis formulas, and extending to trigonometric interpolation and heterogeneous sensor scenarios.
AI.motivation: To unify diverse interpolation problems (Lagrange, Hermite, trigonometric) under a single linear-algebraic framework using dual spaces, decoupling interpolation conditions from basis choices.
AI.method: Represents interpolation conditions as linear functionals in the dual of a finite-dimensional function space; derives explicit dual basis formulas; extends to trigonometric interpolation with closed-form expressions and degeneracy analysis.
AI.result: General existence and uniqueness theorem; closed-form dual basis expressions for trigonometric interpolation; interpolation scheme for heterogeneous sensors (different points for values and derivatives).
AI.conclusion: The framework is systematic, dimension-aware, and versatile, with demonstrated applications in kinematics and periodic function interpolation, suggesting broader use in trajectory planning and signal processing.

title: From Landau Equation and Large Deviations to Efficient Simulations of Dynamical Fluctuations
summary: The (deterministic) Landau equation captures the mean long-term evolution of dynamically hot long-range interacting finite-$N$ systems. Though successful, this kinetic equation fundamentally ignores dynamical fluctuations. Building upon Large Deviation Theory, we present a physically-consistent system of Langevin equations that simultaneously recovers the mean Landau dynamics and accurately captures the corresponding fluctuations among different realizations. We show in particular how these Langevin equations can be derived from Rostoker's principle in the limit of weak two-body deflections. We extensively validate these equations against tailored direct $N$-body simulations, showing an exquisite level of agreement.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the deterministic Landau equation for hot long-range interacting N-body systems by developing Langevin equations that capture both mean dynamics and dynamical fluctuations, validated against direct N-body simulations.
AI.motivation: The deterministic Landau equation captures mean evolution of hot long-range interacting systems but fundamentally ignores dynamical fluctuations, which need to be incorporated for a complete description.
AI.method: Building on Large Deviation Theory, the authors derive a system of Langevin equations from Rostoker's principle in the limit of weak two-body deflections to simultaneously recover mean Landau dynamics and capture fluctuations.
AI.result: The derived Langevin equations show exquisite agreement with direct N-body simulations, accurately capturing both mean dynamics and fluctuations among different realizations.
AI.conclusion: A physically-consistent Langevin framework has been developed that extends the Landau equation to include fluctuations, validated by N-body simulations.

title: One-Step Flow Matching for Generative Modeling of Path-Dependent Physical Fields
summary: Physical simulations for intricate geometries with path-dependent constitutive models face difficulties due to the enormous computational cost they require. Recently, the emergence of generative AI models, which succeed in image and video synthesis tasks, has provided a promise to further improve simulations. Although U-Net-based denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) have been adopted for elastic stress field generation, they typically require hundreds of sampling steps, and applications of generative models to path-dependent, e.g. plastic, stress fields remain very limited. In this work, we propose a novel flow matching (FM) model based on a transformer backbone for high-resolution path-dependent stress field generation with stochastic loading-unloading paths and geometry. The proposed model operates within the latent space of a variational autoencoder (VAE) and formulates the simulation of plastic fields as a video synthesis task, directly generating the stress fields across all time steps. Meanwhile, we design a non-Gaussian source distribution for flow matching, such that crossings among conditional transport paths are reduced during training. This enables our model to generate satisfactory samples in one step without relying on distillation. In addition, we introduce token-level loading embeddings and two auxiliary networks to further enhance the model performance in path-dependent simulation. The results demonstrate that, even with a limited training dataset, our model can accurately generate high-resolution path-dependent fields. It is much more computationally efficient than finite element analysis, providing a speedup of 6 to 7 times over FEM on CPUs and approximately two orders of magnitude speedup on consumer-grade GPUs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a flow matching (FM) model with a transformer backbone and VAE latent space for generating high-resolution path-dependent (plastic) stress fields, achieving 6-7× speedup over FEM on CPUs and ~100× on GPUs, with one-step sampling capability.
AI.motivation: Physical simulations for complex geometries with path-dependent constitutive models (e.g., plastic stress fields) are computationally expensive. Existing U-Net-based DDPMs require hundreds of sampling steps and struggle with path-dependent stress fields.
AI.method: The method uses (1) a transformer backbone with flow matching (FM) in VAE latent space, (2) a non-Gaussian source distribution to reduce path crossings in training, (3) token-level loading embeddings, and (4) two auxiliary networks. It treats plastic field simulation as a video synthesis task.
AI.result: The model accurately generates high-resolution path-dependent stress fields even with limited training data. It achieves 6-7× speedup over FEM on CPUs and approximately two orders of magnitude speedup on consumer-grade GPUs, with one-step generation capability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed flow matching model offers an efficient and accurate alternative to traditional FEM for path-dependent simulations, significantly reducing computational cost while maintaining quality.

title: Prescribed--Energy Connecting Orbits for Quasilinear Conservative Systems
summary: We consider quasilinear conservative systems \[ (φ(|\dot q|)\dot q)'=\nabla V(q), \qquad q\in\R^{N}, \] with $Φ$-growth kinetic term and potential $V\in C^{1}(\R^{N};\R)$. Assuming that for some $c\in\R$ the sublevel set $\{V\le c\}$ splits into two disjoint closed subsets $\mathcal V_c^{-}$ and $\mathcal V_c^{+}$, we prove the existence of trajectories $q_c$ with prescribed energy $-c$ connecting $\mathcal V_c^{-}$ and $\mathcal V_c^{+}$, obtained through an energy-constrained variational method. Although the construction yields weak solutions in an Orlicz-Sobolev setting, minimal $c$-connections are shown to be classical $C^2$ trajectories satisfying the strong energy identity $E_{q_c}\equiv -c$. The resulting entire trajectories include heteroclinic, homoclinic, and brake-type orbits. Applications to double-well, Duffing-type, and multiple pendulum systems are discussed.

AI.tldr: This paper proves existence of classical C² trajectories with prescribed energy connecting disjoint sublevel sets in quasilinear conservative systems with Φ-growth kinetics, using an energy-constrained variational method in Orlicz-Sobolev spaces.
AI.motivation: To establish existence of heteroclinic, homoclinic, and brake-type orbits for quasilinear conservative systems with Φ-growth, where standard variational methods may fail due to the nonlinear kinetic term.
AI.method: Energy-constrained variational method in an Orlicz-Sobolev setting, constructing weak solutions that are then shown to be classical C² trajectories satisfying the strong energy identity.
AI.result: Existence of trajectories connecting disjoint sublevel sets with prescribed energy; solutions are classical C² and satisfy strong energy identity E_q ≡ -c.
AI.conclusion: The method yields entire trajectories including heteroclinic, homoclinic, and brake-type orbits, with applications to double-well, Duffing-type, and multiple pendulum systems.

title: Factored Sparse Approximate Inverse Preconditioning via Spectral Optimization
summary: In this paper, we study value selection for fixed-pattern factorized sparse approximate inverse preconditioners. Given a prescribed sparsity pattern for a factor $G,$ we choose its admissible entries by optimizing spectral objectives of the congruent preconditioned operator $P(G)=GAG^T.$ This differs from classical sparse approximate inverse and FSAI constructions, which choose entries through algebraic Frobenius-residual criteria. For symmetric positive definite systems, the spectral target is a cluster near $+1.$ For symmetric indefinite systems, where congruence preserves inertia, we introduce a bimodal loss that drives positive and negative eigenvalues toward separated clusters near $+1$ and $-1,$ while penalizing eigenvalues near zero. To make these objectives practical for large sparse matrices, we derive projected Krylov support-gradients. Lanczos runs provide both a stochastic trace estimate of the spectral objective and a Ritz approximation to the exact gradient. We implement the resulting gradient through a detached Rayleigh surrogate: the Lanczos data are computed without gradient tracking and held fixed, while the backward pass differentiates only recomputed Rayleigh quotients with respect to the admissible entries of $G.$ This avoids differentiating through the Lanczos recurrence while returning a matrix-free gradient on the prescribed support. We also discuss a projected Kernel Polynomial Method rule as a finite polynomial comparison. Experiments on finite-element test problems show that spectral value selection improves fixed-support preconditioners, especially for symmetric indefinite saddle-point systems. We further demonstrate a graph neural network model for predicting admissible factor entries across related matrices.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes spectral objectives (instead of Frobenius-norm criteria) for selecting entries in fixed-pattern factorized sparse approximate inverse (FSAI) preconditioners, using Lanczos-based projected Krylov support-gradients with a detached Rayleigh surrogate, and demonstrates improvements for symmetric positive definite and indefinite systems, including saddle-point problems.
AI.motivation: Traditional FSAI preconditioners select entries by minimizing Frobenius-residual criteria, which are algebraically convenient but may not optimally cluster eigenvalues for iterative solver convergence. The authors aim to directly optimize spectral objectives to better accelerate Krylov methods.
AI.method: The method uses projected Krylov support-gradients: Lanczos runs provide stochastic trace estimates of the spectral objective and Ritz approximations of the exact gradient. A detached Rayleigh surrogate avoids differentiating through the Lanczos recurrence, computing gradients only through recomputed Rayleigh quotients. For symmetric indefinite systems, a bimodal loss clusters positive/negative eigenvalues near +1/-1 while penalizing near-zero eigenvalues.
AI.result: Experiments on finite-element test problems show that spectral value selection improves fixed-support preconditioners over classical Frobenius-based approaches, particularly for symmetric indefinite saddle-point systems. A GNN model is also demonstrated for predicting factor entries across related matrices.
AI.conclusion: Spectral optimization of fixed-pattern FSAI preconditioners is practically feasible via the proposed gradient method and yields better preconditioners than algebraic Frobenius criteria, especially for indefinite systems. The approach opens possibilities for learning-based preconditioner construction.

title: Crust glass formation reveals the neutron star birth properties in IGR J17480-2446
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures, accepted in Phys. Rev. Letters
summary: IGR J17480-2446 is a low-mass X-ray binary, harboring an exceptional accreting pulsar (a neutron star) with an unusual spin frequency of 11 Hz and a very slow post-outburst crust cooling. The former may imply that it is observed at an early stage of recycling, while the latter was shown to indicate the presence in the outer crust of a low thermal conductivity layer, possibly made of glass. Here we argue that the glass layer formation is a natural result of accretion induced failure of pristine cold crystal crust. This allows us to determine the mass of the accreted material as $ΔM \approx 2.4\times 10^{-6}~M_\odot$, confirming very early accretion stage for this neutron star. An analysis of spin and thermal state reveals a peculiar set of neutron star birth properties which is commonly associated with `recycled' neutron stars, i.e.\ those that have been experiencing prolonged periods of accretion from a companion. We speculate that such birth properties may represent the outcome of neutron star formation in an electron-capture supernova.

AI.tldr: IGR J17480-2446 is a neutron star with unusual low spin and slow cooling; this study explains the glassy crust layer as a result of accretion-induced crystal failure, revealing its early accretion stage and suggesting formation via electron-capture supernova.
AI.motivation: To understand the unusual properties (11 Hz spin, slow cooling) of the neutron star IGR J17480-2446 and explain the formation of a low thermal conductivity glass layer in its crust.
AI.method: The authors argue that the glass layer forms naturally from accretion-induced failure of the pristine cold crystal crust, using this to estimate accreted mass and analyze spin/thermal states.
AI.result: Determined accreted mass ΔM ≈ 2.4×10⁻⁶ M☉, confirming very early accretion stage; identified peculiar birth properties associated with recycled neutron stars.
AI.conclusion: The neutron star is at an early recycling stage, and its birth properties may be consistent with formation in an electron-capture supernova.

title: Evolutionary Optimization Reveals Structural Constraints on Reservoir Architecture for Spatiotemporal Chaos
summary: Biological systems maintain function in fluctuating environments by transforming past stimulation into internal dynamical states that support future-oriented responses. Reservoir computing provides a computational analogue, but standard formulations often treat the recurrent substrate as a fixed random network and train only the readout. Here we ask how the substrate itself changes when reservoir architecture is placed under evolutionary selection for prediction. Using the Kuramoto--Sivashinsky equation as a testbed for spatiotemporal chaos, we evolved reservoirs over five construction hyperparameters: size, connectivity degree, spectral radius, input scaling, and readout regularization. Evolution reduced prediction error at the population level, extended the low-error forecast horizon, and organized the design space along a diminishing-return size--efficiency frontier. Structural analyses showed that evolved reservoirs remained within a conserved stochastic-block-model-like spectral envelope while refining low-eigenvalue modes, locking modularity to an intermediate band, and pruning connection cost within that band. Pareto analysis showed that elite reservoirs occupied a horizontal floor in the cost--modularity plane, indicating that accuracy and efficiency were achieved jointly rather than through a simple trade-off. These findings show that evolutionary optimization does not merely improve prediction, but exposes interpretable structural constraints on the recurrent substrate: it stabilizes a task-suitable dynamical class and refines the architectural degrees of freedom most relevant for prediction. Evolutionary reservoir computing therefore provides a bio-inspired framework for studying how predictive demands shape adaptive dynamical networks.

AI.tldr: This paper evolves reservoir computing architectures for predicting chaotic spatiotemporal dynamics (Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation) and finds that evolution improves prediction, extends forecast horizon, and reveals interpretable structural constraints—including a conserved spectral envelope, intermediate modularity band, and a cost-efficiency Pareto floor where accuracy and efficiency are jointly achieved.
AI.motivation: To understand how recurrent substrate (reservoir) architecture changes under evolutionary pressure for prediction, drawing inspiration from biological systems that transform past stimuli into internal dynamical states for future-oriented responses.
AI.method: Evolved reservoir computers over five hyperparameters (size, connectivity degree, spectral radius, input scaling, readout regularization) using the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation as a spatiotemporal chaos testbed, then performed structural and Pareto analyses on the evolved populations.
AI.result: Evolution reduced prediction error and extended forecast horizon; reservoirs maintained a conserved spectral envelope while refining low-eigenvalue modes, locked modularity to an intermediate band, and pruned connection cost within that band. Elite reservoirs occupied a Pareto-optimal horizontal floor in cost-modularity space.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary optimization exposes interpretable structural constraints on recurrent substrates—it stabilizes a task-suitable dynamical class and refines the most relevant architectural degrees of freedom. This provides a bio-inspired framework for understanding how predictive demands shape adaptive dynamical networks.

title: Failure of zero extension in parabolic Sobolev spaces
summary: We show that spatial zero extension across the boundary may fail in parabolic Sobolev spaces $\mathring{\mathcal{H}}^1_p((0,T) \times Ω)$, which can also be characterized as $$ L_p(0,T;\mathring{W}^1_p(Ω))\cap W^1_p(0,T; W^{-1}_{p}(Ω)). $$ More precisely, for any $p\in [1, \infty)$, we construct a function $u\in \mathring{\mathcal{H}}^1_p((0,T)\times \mathbb{R}^d_+)$ whose zero extension does not belong to $\mathcal{H}^1_p((0,T)\times \mathbb{R}^d)$. The obstruction occurs even for a flat boundary and is caused by a self-similar boundary layer concentrated at the initial-boundary corner, which produces a boundary supported normal flux defect after zero extension. We also discuss the suitability of various Sobolev-type spaces as solution spaces for parabolic equations in divergence form.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that zero extension across boundaries fails for parabolic Sobolev spaces due to a self-similar boundary layer at the initial-boundary corner creating a normal flux defect, and discusses suitable solution spaces for divergence-form parabolic equations.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that spatial zero extension across boundaries is not valid in parabolic Sobolev spaces $\mathring{\mathcal{H}}^1_p$, and to examine appropriate function spaces for solving parabolic equations in divergence form.
AI.method: Constructs counterexample functions $u\in \mathring{\mathcal{H}}^1_p((0,T)\times \mathbb{R}^d_+)$ for any $p\in[1,\infty)$ whose zero extension fails to belong to $\mathcal{H}^1_p((0,T)\times \mathbb{R}^d)$. The obstruction arises from a self-similar boundary layer localized at the initial-boundary corner.
AI.result: Zero extension fails even for flat boundaries; the obstruction is due to a boundary-supported normal flux defect caused by self-similar boundary layer behavior at the initial-boundary corner.
AI.conclusion: Standard zero extension across boundaries is not valid for these parabolic spaces, necessitating careful consideration of suitable Sobolev-type spaces as solution spaces for parabolic equations.

title: Locally conservative redistribution limiting and applications to the approximation of conservation equations, Part II
summary: A non-intrusive, discretization and PDE agnostic limiting technique is introduced. The method is conservative and can be applied to high-order approximations including spectral methods. It can be applied to time-dependent and time-independent problems. It can be used for elliptic, parabolic and hyperbolic problems.

AI.tldr: A conservative, non-intrusive limiting technique for high-order approximations that works across various discretizations, PDE types, and problem categories.
AI.motivation: To develop a limiting technique that is agnostic to discretization methods and PDE types, applicable broadly without intrusive modifications.
AI.method: A non-intrusive, conservative limiting approach applicable to high-order approximations, including spectral methods, for both time-dependent and time-independent problems.
AI.result: The method is conservative and works for elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic problems across various discretizations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed technique offers a versatile, PDE-agnostic solution for enforcing conservation in high-order numerical methods.

title: Analytical calculation of the spectrum of nonlinear Compton scattering beyond local approximations
summary: We derive compact analytical formulae for the spectrum of nonlinear Compton scattering in a finite plane-wave pulse with a smooth temporal envelope. The strong-field QED probability is reduced to finite-pulse phase integrals, which are evaluated asymptotically for multicycle pulses with a broad class of smooth envelopes. We use the uniform approximation to remove the caustic divergences that appear at the nonlinear edges of broadened harmonics. Away from the caustics, it reduces to the standard saddle-point result. The behavior near the linear edge is further improved by an envelope-corrected saddle-point approximation. The approach retains the harmonic substructure in the spectral-angular region carrying the dominant part of the emitted radiation. The locally monochromatic approximation is recovered by averaging the finite-pulse interference. Within their asymptotic domain of applicability, the resulting formulae agree with direct numerical calculations and can be used to evaluate spectra from an electron beam.

AI.tldr: Derives compact analytical formulas for nonlinear Compton scattering spectra in finite plane-wave pulses using asymptotic methods, uniform approximation, and envelope-corrected saddle-point techniques.
AI.motivation: To obtain tractable analytical expressions for nonlinear Compton scattering spectra in finite pulses, avoiding caustic divergences and retaining harmonic substructure for efficient beam calculations.
AI.method: Reduces strong-field QED probability to finite-pulse phase integrals, evaluates asymptotically for multicycle pulses with smooth envelopes, uses uniform approximation to remove caustic divergences, and applies envelope-corrected saddle-point approximation near linear edges.
AI.result: Formulae agree with direct numerical calculations within their asymptotic domain; locally monochromatic approximation is recovered by averaging finite-pulse interference.
AI.conclusion: The derived compact formulae can be used to evaluate spectra from an electron beam, providing a practical tool for strong-field QED calculations.

title: Isometrization of Tensor Network States via Gauge Propagation
summary: We introduce a gauge-propagation approach for approximately converting generic tensor-network states into an isometric tensor-network state form with a prescribed orthogonality center. In one dimension, this propagation is exact because the non-isometric factor produced by a QR or singular-value decomposition is supported on a single virtual bond. In higher-dimensional networks, however, a local step can have several outgoing directions, and the residual factor is generally not separable into independent single-bond contributions. We address this local obstruction by approximating a local tensor, or a contracted local cluster, by structured terms consisting of an isometric factor multiplied by a tensor product of output-leg factors. The isometric factor is retained at the current site or cluster, while the output-leg factors are absorbed into neighboring tensors along the propagation directions. This construction provides a local truncation criterion for gauge propagation and a practical route to refinement by increasing the number of retained terms or enlarging the local cluster. Benchmarks on random tensors and on the loop-gas tensor representation of the Kitaev spin liquid show that this refinement reduces both local residuals and accumulated propagation errors. For the loop-gas tensor, two structured terms reduce the local residual to numerical precision, and enlarging the local object from 2-in-2-out to 4-in-2-out and 6-in-2-out clusters lowers both local truncation errors and accumulated errors in finite honeycomb gauge propagation. These results identify propagation-compatible local decomposition as a useful building block for approximate isometrization and as a potential initializer or preconditioner for variational isoTNS algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a gauge-propagation approach to convert generic tensor-network states into isometric tensor-network states with a prescribed orthogonality center. It addresses the challenge of non-separable residual factors in higher-dimensional networks by approximating local tensors with structured isometric terms. Benchmarks on random tensors and Kitaev spin liquid models show that refinement reduces local residuals and propagation errors, making this method useful for initialization or preconditioning in variational isoTNS algorithms.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a practical method for converting generic tensor-network states into isometric form (with orthogonality center) for higher-dimensional networks, where exact gauge propagation fails because residual factors from local decompositions are generally not separable into independent single-bond contributions.
AI.method: The method introduces gauge propagation with local approximation: each local tensor (or contracted cluster) is approximated by structured terms consisting of an isometric factor multiplied by a tensor product of output-leg factors. The isometric factor is kept locally, while output-leg factors are propagated to neighboring tensors. Refinement is achieved by increasing the number of retained terms or enlarging the local cluster size.
AI.result: Benchmarks on random tensors and the loop-gas tensor representation of the Kitaev spin liquid show that refinement reduces both local residuals and accumulated propagation errors. For the loop-gas tensor, two structured terms suffice to reduce the local residual to numerical precision. Enlarging clusters from 2-in-2-out to 4-in-2-out and 6-in-2-out further lowers truncation and accumulated errors.
AI.conclusion: This propagation-compatible local decomposition is a useful building block for approximate isometrization and can serve as an effective initializer or preconditioner for variational isoTNS algorithms.

title: Quantitative homogenization of convex Hamilton-Jacobi equations in the Wasserstein space
summary: We study a homogenization problem for first-order Hamilton-Jacobi equations in the Wasserstein space with a convex Hamiltonian. We show that the solution $U^\varepsilon$, which is the value function of a mean field control problem, converges uniformly as $\varepsilon \to 0$ to the solution of a limiting Hamilton-Jacobi equation whose Hamiltonian is obtained through a suitable cell problem. Furthermore, we establish quantitative rates of convergence. Under general assumptions with multiscale dependence, we prove that the rate of convergence is $O(\sqrt{\varepsilon})$. When the Hamiltonian depends only on the fast variable and the momentum, we establish the sharp convergence rate $O(\varepsilon)$. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first quantitative convergence result extending the optimal rate for first-order Hamilton-Jacobi equations in finite dimensions to the Wasserstein space. Finally, we show that our analysis extends to dynamic optimal transport problems, where the terminal condition imposes a constraint on the final distribution.

AI.tldr: This paper studies homogenization of Hamilton-Jacobi equations in the Wasserstein space, proving the solution converges uniformly to a limiting equation with convergence rates: O(√ε) generally and sharp O(ε) under specific conditions, extending finite-dimensional optimal rates to this infinite-dimensional setting.
AI.motivation: To establish quantitative convergence rates for homogenization of Hamilton-Jacobi equations in the infinite-dimensional Wasserstein space, which is a novel extension of known finite-dimensional results.
AI.method: The authors analyze the value function of a mean field control problem, perform a cell problem to derive the effective Hamiltonian, and prove uniform convergence with quantitative rates using convexity assumptions and multiscale analysis.
AI.result: General convergence rate O(√ε); sharp convergence rate O(ε) when Hamiltonian depends only on fast variable and momentum; first quantitative convergence result extending optimal finite-dimensional rates to Wasserstein space.
AI.conclusion: The homogenization framework extends to dynamic optimal transport problems with terminal distribution constraints, establishing a foundation for quantitative analysis in Wasserstein space.

title: Computation of the Fourier transform for a continuous integrable function via NFFT
summary:
We investigate the approximation of continuous Fourier transforms by
  trigonometric sampling polynomials and their efficient evaluation by the
  nonequispaced fast Fourier transform (NFFT). While the NFFT is traditionally
  used for the evaluation of trigonometric polynomials, we show that it can also
  serve as an effective computational tool for the approximation of Fourier
  transform values.
  Building on ideas of M.~Ehler, K.~Gröchenig, and A.~Klotz \cite{EhGrKl24}, we derive explicit
  $\ell_\infty$ error bounds between the Fourier transform and suitable sampling
  polynomials. The resulting estimates quantify the influence of the sampling
  width and truncation parameter and provide rigorous accuracy guarantees on
  entire frequency intervals. In contrast to previous analyses focusing mainly on
  discrete or $L_2$-type errors, our results yield uniform approximation bounds
  that are directly relevant for practical computations.
  The derived theory leads to a simple algorithmic framework: first approximate
  the Fourier transform by a trigonometric sampling polynomial and then evaluate this
  polynomial efficiently by the NFFT. Numerical experiments confirm the
  theoretical convergence rates and demonstrate that accurate approximations of
  continuous Fourier transforms can be obtained with moderate computational
  effort.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that the nonequispaced fast Fourier transform (NFFT) can effectively approximate continuous Fourier transforms via trigonometric sampling polynomials, providing uniform ℓ∞ error bounds and a practical algorithmic framework validated by numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous uniform (ℓ∞) error bounds for approximating continuous Fourier transforms using NFFT, moving beyond existing discrete or L₂-type analyses, and to quantify the influence of sampling width and truncation parameters.
AI.method: The method first approximates the continuous Fourier transform by a trigonometric sampling polynomial, then evaluates this polynomial efficiently using the NFFT. The approach builds on prior work by Ehler, Gröchenig, and Klotz, deriving explicit ℓ∞ error bounds.
AI.result: Derived explicit ℓ∞ error bounds between the Fourier transform and sampling polynomials, quantifying the effects of sampling width and truncation parameter. Numerical experiments confirm theoretical convergence rates and demonstrate accurate approximations with moderate computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The NFFT is an effective computational tool not just for evaluating trigonometric polynomials but also for approximating continuous Fourier transforms, with rigorous uniform accuracy guarantees across entire frequency intervals.

title: Phase-mixing of acoustic waves with applications to solar tachocline
summary: We adapt the \textit{magnetohydrodynamic} wave phase-mixing paradigm [Tsiklauri et al. (2003)] to investigate \textit{acoustic} wave propagation and damping in media where transverse sound speed gradients exist. Using an analytical model, we recover previous harmonic wave and Gaussian pulse evolution solutions now controlled by the spatial gradient of the local \textit{sound speed}. We discover a scaling law governing a Harris current sheet-like pulse evolution: under developed-stage phase-mixing, the peak envelope of such pulse amplitude scales with propagation distance as a new power-law $\max(P_1) \propto x^{-9/2}$. Applying our model to the solar tachocline directly resolves the 26-year-old helioseismic mystery of low-$\ell$ global $p$-mode linewidth anomalies observed by BiSON above $ν\approx 3000\,μ\text{Hz}$. We demonstrate that the sound speed gradient forces rapid, non-turbulent energy damping directly in the shear zone, providing the exact high-efficiency bulk dissipation needed to account for the missing energy sink deep within the solar tacholine. Our model provides the exact damping rates that classical, homogeneous models severely underestimated in the past. Finally, our results provide actionable design strategies for engineering compact stealth coatings or meshes to achieve enhanced acoustic signature suppression from moving bodies immersed in a fluid.

AI.tldr: This paper adapts MHD wave phase-mixing to acoustic waves with transverse sound speed gradients, discovers a new power-law scaling for pulse evolution, resolves long-standing helioseismic linewidth anomalies in the solar tachocline, and proposes applications for acoustic stealth coatings.
AI.motivation: To explain the 26-year-old mystery of low-ℓ global p-mode linewidth anomalies observed by BiSON above ~3000 μHz, which classical homogeneous models severely underestimated, and to extend the phase-mixing paradigm from MHD to purely acoustic waves.
AI.method: Adapts the MHD wave phase-mixing framework to acoustic waves in media with transverse sound speed gradients. Uses an analytical model to derive harmonic wave and Gaussian pulse evolution, revealing a new power-law scaling (max(P₁) ∝ x⁻⁹/²) for Harris current sheet-like pulse evolution under developed phase-mixing.
AI.result: The model shows that sound speed gradient forces rapid, non-turbulent energy damping in the solar tachocline shear zone, providing the missing high-efficiency bulk dissipation. This directly resolves the BiSON linewidth anomalies. A new scaling law is discovered: max(P₁) ∝ x⁻⁹/².
AI.conclusion: The findings provide exact damping rates previously underestimated by classical models and offer actionable design strategies for engineering compact stealth coatings or meshes to enhance acoustic signature suppression from moving bodies in fluids.

title: Scattering Observables from Few-Body Densities and Application in Light Nuclei
comment: Dissertation directed by Harald W. Griesshammer
summary:
The Transition Density Amplitude (TDA) method of Griesshammer et al. is applied to compute scattering observables for Compton scattering, threshold neutral pion photoproduction, and elastic pion scattering on the light nuclei Hydrogen 3, Helium 3, Helium 4, and Lithium 6. In this formalism the amplitude factorizes into an irreducible few-body kernel, which encodes the interaction of the probe with the active nucleons, and a transition density amplitude, which carries the nuclear structure information. Because the densities are computed once per nucleus and momentum transfer and then convolved with any process-specific kernel, the same nuclear input is reused across reactions, yielding substantial computational savings over direct evaluation. This factorization is realized in the publicly available Fortran suite DensityScattering, developed as part of the present work; a researcher implementing a new reaction need only supply the corresponding kernel in a prescribed format, with infrastructure for density handling, integration, quantum-number summation, and output already provided.
  The TDAs are constructed from nuclear wave functions using the semilocal momentum-space regularized chiral potential of Reinert, Krebs, and Epelbaum at cutoffs of 450 and 500 MeV. For Lithium 6, no-core shell model wave functions are evolved under a Similarity Renormalization Group (SRG) transformation; a back-transformation scheme ("SRG-And-Back"), co-developed for the present work, returns the densities to the physical momentum scale and thereby preserves the original chiral ordering. The induced uncertainty is validated against exact Helium 4 results at the 2% level, and convergence studies for Lithium 6 Compton scattering bound the residual uncertainty below 6%.

AI.tldr: This paper applies the Transition Density Amplitude (TDA) method to compute scattering observables for Compton scattering, pion photoproduction, and elastic pion scattering on light nuclei (³H, ³He, ⁴He, ⁶Li). The method factorizes the amplitude into a process-specific kernel and a reusable nuclear transition density. A publicly available Fortran suite (DensityScattering) is introduced. Nuclear wave functions use chiral potentials with SRG evolution and back-transformation for ⁶Li. Uncertainties are validated at 2% for ⁴He and below 6% for ⁶Li.
AI.motivation: To enable computationally efficient computation of multiple scattering observables across different light nuclei by factorizing nuclear structure information (transition densities) from reaction-specific physics, avoiding redundant direct evaluations for each reaction.
AI.method: The Transition Density Amplitude (TDA) formalism factorizes the scattering amplitude into an irreducible few-body kernel (probe-nucleon interaction) and a transition density (nuclear structure). TDAs are built from wave functions using semilocal momentum-space regularized chiral potentials (cutoffs 450, 500 MeV). For ⁶Li, no-core shell model wave functions are SRG-evolved, then back-transformed ("SRG-And-Back") to physical scale. The DensityScattering Fortran suite implements this framework.
AI.result: The factorization approach yields substantial computational savings. The SRG back-transformation uncertainty is validated against exact ⁴He results at the 2% level. Convergence studies for ⁶Li Compton scattering bound residual uncertainty below 6%.
AI.conclusion: The TDA method successfully enables efficient, reusable computation of scattering observables across multiple reactions on light nuclei, with controlled uncertainties. The DensityScattering suite makes this framework publicly available for researchers to implement new reactions by supplying only the process-specific kernel.

title: How to Cook a Soft-Boiled Egg Optimally: A Laplace-Transform Solution of a Two-Domain Heat Equation
summary: We study the problem of cooking the yolk and albumen of a hen's egg to their respective optimal temperatures of $T_Y^* = 65^\circ$C and $T_W^* = 85^\circ$C, subject to the requirement that neither temperature ever exceed its target at any time during cooking, since temporary overshoot still overcooks the egg even if the final reading is correct. We model the egg as a two-domain sphere with distinct thermal diffusivities, and take the Laplace transform of the heat equation in each domain, reducing the problem to a $3 \times 3$ linear system in the transform variable $s$ with hyperbolic-trigonometric solutions. The resulting transform is inverted numerically via Talbot's method and validated against a finite-difference solver. A single boiling phase cannot satisfy the no-overshoot requirement: the thin outer albumen heats far faster than the insulated yolk and necessarily overshoots $T_W^*$ before the yolk approaches $T_Y^*$. We show that a three-phase protocol resolves this: a sous-vide pre-soak at exactly $65^\circ$C (which cannot overshoot since the bath temperature equals the target), a short boil to bring the albumen toward $T_W^*$, and an ice-water bath that arrests the albumen's residual overshoot while residual heat continues raising the yolk to its target. Optimizing the phase durations gives $17.26$ minutes of sous-vide, $66$ seconds of boiling, and an ice bath, achieving both targets at $T^* \approx 20.67$ minutes with neither constraint violated at any time. This compares favorably with the periodic protocol of Di Lorenzo et al. (2025), which requires 32 minutes and misses both targets substantially.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a three-phase cooking protocol (sous-vide at 65°C, short boil, ice bath) to simultaneously cook egg yolk and albumen to their optimal temperatures (65°C and 85°C) without overshoot, achieving both targets at ~20.67 minutes—significantly better than a 32-minute periodic protocol that misses both targets.
AI.motivation: To cook both egg yolk and albumen to their respective optimal temperatures (65°C and 85°C) without any temperature overshoot during the process, since temporary overshoot still overcooks the egg even if final temperatures are correct.
AI.method: Models the egg as a two-domain sphere with distinct thermal diffusivities; solves heat equations via Laplace transform, reduces to a 3×3 linear system with hyperbolic-trigonometric solutions; inverts numerically using Talbot's method; validates against finite-difference solver.
AI.result: A three-phase protocol (sous-vide pre-soak at 65°C, short boil of 66 seconds, ice-water bath) achieves both targets at 17.26 min (sous-vide) + 66 sec (boil) + ice bath = ~20.67 minutes without violating the no-overshoot constraint. The periodic protocol of Di Lorenzo et al. requires 32 minutes and misses both targets substantially.
AI.conclusion: A single boiling phase cannot satisfy the no-overshoot requirement because the thin albumen heats faster than the insulated yolk. The proposed three-phase protocol solves this problem efficiently, outperforming existing periodic cooking approaches.

title: The Bojanov--Naidenov inequality for quartics and second derivatives
summary:
We settle the case $n=4$, $k=2$ of the Bojanov--Naidenov problem for algebraic polynomials. Let $P$ be a real polynomial of degree at most four with $\left\lVert{P}\right\rVert_{C[-1,1]}\leq 1$, and let $T_4(x)=8x^4-8x^2+1$. We prove that, for every $t\geq0$, \[
  \int_{-1}^{1} \bigl(|P''(x)|-t\bigr)_+\,dx
  \leq
  \int_{-1}^{1} \bigl(|T_4''(x)|-t\bigr)_+\,dx . \] This tail estimate implies \[
  \int_{-1}^{1}\varphi(|P''(x)|)\,dx
  \leq
  \int_{-1}^{1}\varphi(|T_4''(x)|)\,dx \] for every nondecreasing convex function $\varphi:[0,\infty)\to\mathbb{R}$. If $\varphi$ is strictly increasing and convex, equality can occur only for $P=\pm T_4$. The proof is elementary and finite. We interpolate at the five extremal points of $T_4$; convexity then reduces the problem to the $32$ sign choices at these nodes. At each vertex the second derivative is a quadratic polynomial, so the remaining work is an explicit comparison of level sets.

AI.tldr: The paper solves the Bojanov-Naidenov problem for n=4, k=2, proving that among all degree-4 real polynomials bounded by 1 on [-1,1], the Chebyshev polynomial T4 maximizes the integral of convex functions of |P''|, with equality only for ±T4.
AI.motivation: To solve a special case (n=4, k=2) of the Bojanov-Naidenov problem, which concerns extremal properties of algebraic polynomials regarding their second derivatives.
AI.method: Interpolates at the five extremal points of T4, uses convexity to reduce to 32 sign choices, and performs explicit comparison of level sets for quadratic polynomials (second derivatives).
AI.result: Proves the integral inequality for tail estimates and for all nondecreasing convex functions φ, with equality only for P = ±T4 when φ is strictly increasing and convex.
AI.conclusion: The case n=4, k=2 of the Bojanov-Naidenov problem is settled with an elementary and finite proof.

title: A kinetic-diffusion Monte Carlo-based particle-level fluid-kinetic decomposition for neutral transport simulations
summary: Neutrals in the plasma edge are commonly modeled by kinetic equations, with quantities of interest given by macroscopic quantities such as density, velocity, and temperature. In reactor-relevant regimes, fully kinetic descriptions solved by Monte Carlo (MC) methods, although accurate, become computationally expensive, whereas fluid-limit approximations are computationally more efficient but may lose accuracy due to boundary effects or low-collisional regimes. Hybrid fluid-kinetic approaches aim to combine the strengths of both descriptions. However, existing simulation methods face challenges, including interface handling in domain decomposition, unphysical assumptions, and iterative coupling in distribution decomposition. In this work, we propose a distribution-decomposition hybrid model constructed at the particle level based on the kinetic-diffusion Monte Carlo (KDMC) method. The model inherits key properties of KDMC: it is asymptotic-preserving and does not require iterative coupling between the fluid and kinetic components. To improve the accuracy of the fluid-part quantities estimation, a Navier-Stokes-type fluid system is derived via Hilbert-Chapman-Enskog expansions, tailored for KDMC. In the considered one-dimensional tests, the resulting fluid system has comparable accuracy to the AFN model used in SOLPS-ITER while requiring substantially fewer nonlinear iterations. Additionally, a tunable reflective boundary condition is introduced that allows balancing accuracy and efficiency. The model exhibits at least 500 times speedup over the kinetic MC, while maintaining relative L2 errors around 10% in a charge exchange (CX)-dominant test case. In non-CX-dominant regimes, the accuracy becomes increasingly sensitive to boundary treatment due to the inherent limitations of the fluid approximation near the boundary, motivating further refinement of the KDMC boundary conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a distribution-decomposition hybrid fluid-kinetic model based on the kinetic-diffusion Monte Carlo (KDMC) method for modeling neutrals in the plasma edge, achieving at least 500x speedup over kinetic MC with ~10% relative L2 errors in CX-dominant regimes.
AI.motivation: Fully kinetic Monte Carlo methods for neutral particles in the plasma edge are accurate but computationally expensive in reactor-relevant regimes, while fluid approximations are efficient but lose accuracy near boundaries or in low-collisional regimes. Existing hybrid approaches face challenges with interface handling, unphysical assumptions, and iterative coupling.
AI.method: A distribution-decomposition hybrid model built at the particle level using the kinetic-diffusion Monte Carlo (KDMC) method, which is asymptotic-preserving and avoids iterative coupling. A Navier-Stokes-type fluid system is derived via Hilbert-Chapman-Enskog expansions for KDMC. A tunable reflective boundary condition is also introduced.
AI.result: The fluid system shows comparable accuracy to the AFN model used in SOLPS-ITER with fewer nonlinear iterations. The model achieves at least 500x speedup over kinetic MC while maintaining ~10% relative L2 errors in CX-dominant tests. In non-CX-dominant regimes, accuracy becomes sensitive to boundary treatment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed KDMC-based hybrid model is effective for CX-dominant regimes, offering significant speedup with acceptable accuracy. However, boundary condition refinement is needed for non-CX-dominant regimes where fluid approximations near boundaries are limiting.

title: Stable blow-up on a sphere for a quadratic-derivative nonlinear wave equation
summary:
We study finite-time blow-up for the nonlinear wave equation \begin{equation*} v_{tt}-Δv=|\nabla_x v|^2 \end{equation*} in dimensions $n\geq2$, under radial symmetry. For every prescribed radius $r_0>0$, we construct solutions which blow up in finite time $T>0$ on the sphere $\{|x|=r_0\}$ with logarithmic Type-I rate. The leading singular dynamics are governed by ``generalised self-similar'' profiles of the associated one-dimensional equation, while the radial geometry generates a curvature correction of size $\mathcal{O}((\frac{T}{r_0})^2)$. A key simplification in our approach is a logarithmic radial correction which removes the first-order radial drift and reduces the geometry to a decaying inverse-square forcing.
  We further prove asymptotic stability of the resulting family under radial perturbations. A new feature compared with the one-dimensional theory is that the stable blow-up family is not fully explicit. To overcome this, we develop spectral and semigroup estimates on an extended light cone, together with Lipschitz dependence on the modulation parameters for the spectral projections, the stable flow, and the non-explicit correction.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs finite-time blow-up solutions for a nonlinear wave equation in dimensions n≥2, where solutions blow up on prescribed spheres with a logarithmic Type-I rate, and proves asymptotic stability under radial perturbations.
AI.motivation: To understand blow-up phenomena for nonlinear wave equations in higher dimensions (n≥2) under radial symmetry, specifically constructing solutions that blow up on prescribed spherical surfaces rather than at a single point.
AI.method: Uses "generalized self-similar" profiles for the 1D equation with a logarithmic radial correction to remove first-order drift, then develops spectral and semigroup estimates on an extended light cone with Lipschitz dependence on modulation parameters.
AI.result: Constructs blow-up solutions on spheres of any prescribed radius with logarithmic Type-I rate, with curvature correction of size O((T/r₀)²). Proves asymptotic stability of the blow-up family under radial perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Establishes existence and stability of finite-time blow-up on spheres for the nonlinear wave equation in dimensions n≥2, despite the blow-up family not being fully explicit, overcoming this via spectral/semigroup analysis.

title: A WKB-based fixed-grid method for capturing trait concentration in a dispersal evolution model
summary: The evolution of dispersal traits is a fundamental topic in evolutionary ecology, where natural selection may drive the trait distribution toward concentration in the rare-mutation regime. This singular behavior poses a serious numerical difficulty, since direct discretizations of the population density require very fine trait grids to identify the fittest trait and to resolve the concentrated profile accurately. In this paper, we develop a WKB-based numerical framework for a dispersal evolution model. By separating the exponentially concentrated trait dependence from a smoother amplitude variable and combining this WKB representation with dual trait-grid implementation and other specially designed techniques, the method recovers the selected trait and the associated concentration structure accurately and efficiently on fixed trait grids. We establish a semi-discrete stationary fixed-grid asymptotic-preserving structure for the rare-mutation limit of the steady-state problem. Numerical experiments compare the proposed method with direct density discretizations and confirm its advantage in the small-mutation regime.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a WKB-based numerical framework to efficiently and accurately solve dispersal evolution models in the rare-mutation regime, where direct density discretizations fail due to extreme trait concentration.
AI.motivation: Natural selection drives dispersal traits to concentrate in the rare-mutation regime, causing severe numerical difficulties for direct discretization methods that require very fine trait grids to resolve the concentrated profile.
AI.method: The method separates the exponentially concentrated trait dependence from a smoother amplitude variable using a WKB representation, combined with dual trait-grid implementation and other techniques, establishing a semi-discrete stationary fixed-grid asymptotic-preserving structure for the rare-mutation limit.
AI.result: The method accurately and efficiently recovers the selected trait and concentration structure on fixed trait grids, outperforming direct density discretizations in the small-mutation regime.
AI.conclusion: The WKB-based numerical framework offers a significant advantage over traditional approaches for solving dispersal evolution models under rare mutation, enabling accurate resolution without excessively fine grids.

title: Projection-Based Reconstruction for Achieving High-Order Accuracy from Low-Order DGSEM Simulations
summary: High-order discontinuous Galerkin spectral element methods (DGSEM) based on Legendre-Gauss-Lobatto (LGL) nodes provide accurate and efficient discretizations for conservation laws. However, their cost, memory footprint, and time-step restrictions increase rapidly when the degree of the polynomial increases. This paper develops a corrected $\mathbb{P}_n\mathbb{P}_m$ ($c\mathbb{P}_n\mathbb{P}_m$) approach for DGSEM-LGL discretizations that aims to recover the accuracy of an $m^{th}$-order approximation while evolving only the degrees of freedom associated with an $n^{th}$-order representation, with $n<m$. The projected evolution of the high-order components is derived first at the continuous level and then in the fully discrete DGSEM-LGL setting. The discrete analysis shows that because LGL quadrature is not exact for the highest Legendre mode, a correction term for that mode is required to preserve the order of convergence. A compact projection-based reconstruction operator is then introduced to recover high-order components without solving the enlarged constrained least-squares systems used in standard reconstruction procedures. For sufficiently smooth solutions, the resulting $c\mathbb{P}_n\mathbb{P}_m$ scheme is shown to achieve the expected $m+1^{th}$ convergence order. Numerical experiments for one- and two-dimensional conservation laws, including Euler, viscous Burgers, and 2D decaying homogeneous isotropic turbulence, confirm theoretical convergence behavior and demonstrate competitive accuracy relative to computational cost, with particularly clear efficiency gains for viscous flows.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a corrected P_nP_m (cP_nP_m) approach for DGSEM-LGL discretizations that achieves high-order accuracy (m+1) while evolving only low-order degrees of freedom (n), with n<m, using a compact projection-based reconstruction operator. It shows that a correction term for the highest Legendre mode is needed due to inexact LGL quadrature, and demonstrates expected convergence and computational efficiency gains, especially for viscous flows.
AI.motivation: High-order DGSEM-LGL methods suffer from rapidly increasing cost, memory, and time-step restrictions as polynomial degree increases. The authors aim to recover high-order accuracy while evolving only low-order degrees of freedom to improve computational efficiency.
AI.method: The authors develop a corrected P_nP_m approach: (1) derive projected evolution of high-order components at continuous level, then in fully discrete DGSEM-LGL setting; (2) identify that inexact LGL quadrature for the highest Legendre mode requires a correction term; (3) introduce a compact projection-based reconstruction operator without solving enlarged constrained least-squares systems.
AI.result: For smooth solutions, the cP_nP_m scheme achieves the expected m+1 convergence order. Numerical experiments on 1D/2D conservation laws (Euler, viscous Burgers, 2D decaying turbulence) confirm theoretical convergence and demonstrate competitive accuracy-per-cost, with clear efficiency gains for viscous flows.
AI.conclusion: The cP_nP_m approach effectively reduces computational cost while preserving high-order accuracy, offering a practical improvement over standard high-order DGSEM-LGL methods, particularly for viscous flow problems.

title: Singular stationary Navier-Stokes flows: examples and stability
summary: Landau solutions, when oriented along the vertical axis, represent a one parameter family of exact, self-similar, axisymmetric, swirl-free solutions to the 3D stationary Navier-Stokes equations forced by an upward facing point-source of momentum at the origin. They have an isolated singularity at the origin. Other singular steady state solutions can be derived from a similar framework. Some of these variants have been proposed as models for physical scenarios. For example, a solution first found by Squire has been proposed as a model of a fluid entrained to a radially discharging surface layer of oil. Another, Serrin's swirling vortex, exhibits qualitative features shared with some tornadoes, like a two-cell structure consisting of a central downdraft and peripheral updraft as well as swirl.
