The first objective of this paper is to provide a detailed analysis of these and other examples, especially when boundaries are present. In this direction we find several new, physically motivated classes of solutions and identify new connections between the physics literature and the mathematics literature. Many of these examples are formulated on the half-space, but much of the mathematical literature on singular steady-state solutions is for the whole-space. The second objective of this paper is to establish asymptotic stability for many of these solutions by formulating the problem in domains with boundaries. Our most general result requires a new approach to asymptotic stability that is based on eventual regularity. As a special case, we prove that a class of steady-state solutions motivated by Serrin's swirling vortex are stable under axisymmetric perturbations. This requires a novel observation because these solutions are too singular -- they are called ``Type III'' in the literature -- to be directly amenable to existing approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes singular solutions to the 3D Navier-Stokes equations (Landau, Squire, Serrin types), finds new solution classes on bounded domains, and establishes asymptotic stability for many, including Serrin's swirling vortex under axisymmetric perturbations, using a novel eventual regularity approach.
AI.motivation: To bridge gaps between physics and mathematics literature on singular steady-state Navier-Stokes solutions, analyze physically motivated examples with boundaries, and establish stability results for highly singular solutions (Type III) that existing methods cannot handle.
AI.method: The paper develops a new asymptotic stability approach based on eventual regularity, formulates problems on domains with boundaries (half-space) rather than whole-space, and provides detailed analytical study of multiple solution families (Landau, Squire, Serrin).
AI.result: Several new physically motivated solution classes are discovered. Asymptotic stability is established for many solutions, notably proving stability of Serrin's swirling vortex under axisymmetric perturbations despite its Type III singularity.
AI.conclusion: New connections between physics and mathematics literature are identified, and a novel stability framework enables analysis of previously intractable singular solutions, advancing understanding of singular steady states in fluid dynamics.

title: Turing-region preservation in matrix-oriented splitting methods for reaction-diffusion systems
summary: We develop matrix-oriented formulations of first-order splitting integrators for two-species reaction-diffusion systems on 2D domains, using the Gierer-Meinhardt system as a benchmark for discrete Turing instability. Exploiting the differential matrix equation associated with tensor-product spatial discretizations, we obtain the families IE-S and EX-S, where the diffusive flow is treated by implicit Euler or exactly, respectively, and the reaction substep is approximated by explicit, symplectic, adjoint-symplectic, Poisson, and explicit-variant local maps. Starting from the continuous diffusion-driven instability threshold, expressed through the modal relation $J_μ= J^* - μD$, we derive fully discrete modal amplification matrices and their Jury conditions. These conditions separate the continuous Turing mechanism, carried by the first Jury condition, from discrete effects carried by the second. Specializing the analysis to the Gierer-Meinhardt model, we exhibit two opposite pathologies. First, in a continuous Turing-stable regime, IMEX may generate a stable spurious pattern through a violation of the second Jury condition. Second, in a real continuous Turing regime, the adjoint-symplectic family may be spuriously stable and suppress the pattern that IMEX correctly detects. For IMEX, whose first Jury condition reproduces exactly the sign of the continuous Turing polynomial, we give an explicit time-step condition guaranteeing preservation of the continuous Turing region, and show that controlling the second Jury condition is needed only in the Turing-stable regime. These examples show that each integrator induces its own discrete Turing region, which should be compared with the continuous one before interpreting numerical patterns; we frame this requirement as preserving a qualitative property of the continuous problem, in the spirit of structure-preserving numerical integration.

AI.tldr: This paper develops matrix-oriented splitting integrators for 2D reaction-diffusion systems (using Gierer-Meinhardt as benchmark) and derives discrete Turing instability conditions via Jury test. It reveals that numerical integrators can create spurious patterns or suppress real ones, framing this as a structure-preserving integration problem.
AI.motivation: To understand how numerical discretization methods affect Turing instability detection in reaction-diffusion systems, and to expose pathologies where integrators either falsely produce or suppress patterns relative to the continuous Turing theory.
AI.method: Matrix-oriented formulations of first-order splitting integrators (IE-S and EX-S families) are developed. Fully discrete modal amplification matrices and Jury conditions are derived from the continuous diffusion-driven instability threshold. The Gierer-Meinhardt model is used to test IMEX, symplectic, adjoint-symplectic, Poisson, and explicit-variant local maps.
AI.result: Two pathologies found: (1) IMEX can generate stable spurious patterns in a Turing-stable regime via violation of the second Jury condition. (2) Adjoint-symplectic family can suppress real patterns that IMEX correctly detects. An explicit time-step condition for IMEX is given to preserve the continuous Turing region.
AI.conclusion: Each integrator induces its own discrete Turing region; numerical patterns must be interpreted by comparing discrete and continuous Turing conditions. This is framed as a qualitative structure-preserving requirement for numerical integration.

title: Multiplicity and solution construction in linear elliptic problems with nonlocal nonlinearities
summary: This work investigates linear elliptic equations with multiple nonlocal nonlinearities in a bounded domain, which takes the form \begin{equation*} -\nabla\cdot(\od\nabla u) +λfu= η\sum_{j=1}^kh_j\boldsymbol{\mathsf{N}}_j[u]+h_0. \end{equation*} Here $λ$ and $η$ are positive parameters, and $\boldsymbol{\mathsf{N}}_j[u]$ represents a nonlocal term dependent on the unknown solution~$u$. All coefficients are defined in the context of a wide range of applications. As such equations generally lack a variational structure, a new approach is developed that combines fixed-point arguments with asymptotic techniques. This method establishes the existence and multiplicity of solutions under specific conditions. Of particular interest is the role of the nonlocal nonlinearities, which lead to diverse structures of solutions. To the best of our knowledge, this property is a new finding not typically observed in related nonlocal elliptic problems.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a novel fixed-point and asymptotic approach to analyze linear elliptic equations with multiple nonlocal nonlinearities, revealing new solution multiplicity and structural diversity not seen in typical nonlocal problems.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of variational structure in linear elliptic equations with multiple nonlocal nonlinearities and to understand the role of these nonlinearities in shaping solution diversity.
AI.method: Combines fixed-point arguments with asymptotic techniques to prove existence and multiplicity of solutions.
AI.result: Established existence and multiplicity of solutions under specific conditions, revealing diverse solution structures driven by nonlocal nonlinearities.
AI.conclusion: Nonlocal nonlinearities induce new structural properties in solutions not previously observed in related nonlocal elliptic problems.

title: On Runge-Kutta convolution quadrature based fractional variational integrators
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2510.11082
summary: Lagrangian systems subject to fractional damping can be incorporated into a variational framework by doubling the state variables and introducing fractional derivatives. Fractional variational integrators based on backward-differentiation convolution quadrature (BD-FCQ), combined with higher-order Galerkin methods, saturate at second-order accuracy because the multistep structure of BDFCQ does not take into account the internal stages of the Galerkin discretization. The main objective of this paper is to develop fractional variational integrators (FVIs) by combining Runge-Kutta convolution quadrature (RKCQ) for the approximation of fractional derivatives with higher-order Galerkin methods. The RKCQ approach is naturally compatible with such stage-based discretizations and is therefore better suited for the construction of higher-order schemes. We are particularly interested in the CQ based on Lobatto IIIC. Preservation properties such as energy decay, as well as convergence properties, are investigated numerically and proved for second-order schemes. The presented schemes reach 2nd, 4th and 6th order of accuracy. A brief discussion on the midpoint fractional integrator is also included.

AI.tldr: This paper develops higher-order fractional variational integrators by combining Runge-Kutta convolution quadrature with Galerkin methods, achieving up to 6th order accuracy while preserving energy decay properties.
AI.motivation: Existing fractional variational integrators based on BDFCQ saturate at second-order accuracy because the multistep structure ignores internal stages of Galerkin discretization. Higher-order schemes are needed.
AI.method: Combines Runge-Kutta convolution quadrature (RKCQ) for fractional derivative approximation with higher-order Galerkin methods. Specifically uses Lobatto IIIC-based CQ, which is naturally compatible with stage-based discretizations.
AI.result: Schemes achieve 2nd, 4th, and 6th order accuracy. Preservation properties (energy decay) and convergence are numerically investigated and proved for second-order schemes.
AI.conclusion: RKCQ-based approach overcomes the order saturation limitation of BDFCQ-based methods, enabling higher-order fractional variational integrators with provable convergence and energy dissipation properties.

title: Uncountably many non-rotationally symmetric type II ancient Yamabe flows on the sphere
summary:
For every $n \ge 3$, we construct uncountably many families of type II ancient solutions to the Yamabe flow on the unit round $n$-sphere $\Ss^n$. These families are pairwise distinct up to conformal equivalence, and no member is conformally equivalent to a rotationally symmetric solution. At every negative time, the Ricci curvature tensor of each solution is indefinite at some point. Moreover, the associated backward limit space is a wedge sum of finitely many isometric copies of $\Ss^n$.
  These examples show that the collection of ancient Yamabe flows on $\Ss^n$ has a much richer structure than suggested by two natural comparison problems: the compact ancient Ricci flows on $\Ss^2$, all of which are known to be rotationally symmetric, and the elliptic Yamabe equation on $\R^n$, whose positive entire solutions are only the standard bubbles.
  The construction uses a non-radial inner--outer gluing scheme. After stereographic projection, we reformulate the flow as a conformally invariant parabolic problem on $\R^n$. By exploiting Kelvin invariance and switching between the Euclidean and spherical formulations as needed, we control the non-radial modes directly without reducing the problem to one space dimension. Weighted Hölder estimates provide the pointwise control needed to establish the Type II behavior, the Ricci-sign property, conformal inequivalence, and the description of the backward limits in a straightforward manner.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs uncountably many families of type II ancient solutions to the Yamabe flow on the unit round n-sphere (n≥3) that are non-rotationally symmetric, have indefinite Ricci curvature, and exhibit rich backward limit structure, vastly expanding the known landscape of ancient Yamabe flows beyond what symmetry-based or elliptic analogies would suggest.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that ancient Yamabe flows on S^n have a much richer structure than previously understood—specifically, unlike ancient Ricci flows on S^2 (all rotationally symmetric) or the elliptic Yamabe equation on R^n (only standard bubbles), there exist many non-radial, conformally inequivalent ancient solutions.
AI.method: A non-radial inner-outer gluing scheme on R^n after stereographic projection, reformulating the flow as a conformally invariant parabolic problem. Kelvin invariance is exploited, switching between Euclidean and spherical formulations. Weighted Hölder estimates provide pointwise control for establishing Type II behavior, Ricci-sign properties, conformal inequivalence, and backward limits.
AI.result: For every n≥3, uncountably many families of type II ancient Yamabe flows on S^n exist, pairwise conformally inequivalent and not rotationally symmetric. At negative times, Ricci curvature is indefinite at some point, and backward limits are wedge sums of finitely many isometric copies of S^n.
AI.conclusion: The collection of ancient Yamabe flows is far richer than previously expected, breaking symmetry and elliptic analogies. The construction provides a flexible framework for building non-radial ancient solutions with controlled geometric properties.

title: A Compact Two-Stage Fourth-Order Two-Derivative IMEX Method with Mixed Compatibility, High Implicit-Solve Efficiency,and Enhanced Stiff Decay
summary: For additively split stiff evolution problems, classical fourth-order IMEX Runge-Kutta methods usually require several stages and complicated coupling order conditions. This paper proposes a compact fourth-order IMEX-type method based on a two-derivative formulation and analyzes its accuracy and stability properties. The proposed scheme incorporates the mixed explicit-implicit interaction directly through temporal derivatives evaluated along the full vector field, which ensures mixed compatibility for non-commuting split systems. With only one intermediate stage and two implicit solves per time step, the method achieves fourth-order accuracy while improving the accuracy obtained per implicit solve compared with classical multi-stage fourth-order IMEX-RK schemes. In addition, the method exhibits stronger damping of stiff modes in the purely implicit scalar limit and in the strong implicit-stiffness limit with a fixed explicit component. In the purely implicit scalar limit, its stability factor decays quadratically as the stiffness increases, whereas a representative classical fourth-order IMEX-RK reference method shows only linear decay. Numerical experiments on non-commuting split systems, scalar stiff-mode damping, increasing stiffness tests, and one- and two-dimensional advection-diffusion high-mode problems confirm the mixed consistency, the predicted stiff decay, and the smaller errors obtained under equal implicit-solve budgets in strongly stiff regimes.

AI.tldr: A compact fourth-order IMEX method based on two-derivative formulation with only one intermediate stage and two implicit solves per time step, achieving fourth-order accuracy with better stiff damping than classical IMEX-RK schemes.
AI.motivation: Classical fourth-order IMEX Runge-Kutta methods for additively split stiff evolution problems require many stages and complicated coupling order conditions, motivating the need for a more efficient and compact scheme.
AI.method: Proposes a compact fourth-order IMEX-type method using a two-derivative formulation that incorporates mixed explicit-implicit interaction through temporal derivatives evaluated along the full vector field, ensuring mixed compatibility for non-commuting split systems. It uses only one intermediate stage and two implicit solves per time step.
AI.result: The method achieves fourth-order accuracy with improved accuracy per implicit solve compared to classical multi-stage fourth-order IMEX-RK schemes. It shows stronger damping of stiff modes - the stability factor decays quadratically (vs. linear decay for classical methods). Numerical experiments confirm mixed consistency, predicted stiff decay, and smaller errors under equal implicit-solve budgets in stiff regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-derivative IMEX method is more efficient and stable than classical fourth-order IMEX-RK schemes, particularly for strongly stiff problems, offering better accuracy per implicit solve and superior stiff-mode damping.

title: Global Well-Posedness of sessile drop problem: 2D Navier-Stokes Flow
summary: We prove the global well-posedness of small perturbations of two-dimensional sessile droplet equilibria for the incompressible Navier--Stokes equations with surface tension, Navier-slip boundary conditions, and a dynamic contact-point law. The main difficulty is the construction of solutions in the presence of the horizontal translational degeneracy of the equilibrium manifold. To remove this degeneracy, we work in a moving polar coordinate system determined by an orthogonality condition. We then establish local well-posedness through a Galerkin construction of pressureless weak solutions, recovery of the pressure, higher-order estimates, and a contraction argument. Combining this local theory with the global energy--dissipation estimates obtained in our previous work yields a unique global solution and the corresponding exponential decay estimate.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global well-posedness for small perturbations of 2D sessile droplet equilibria under Navier-Stokes with surface tension, Navier-slip conditions, and dynamic contact-point law, using a moving polar coordinate system to handle translational degeneracy.
AI.motivation: To address the horizontal translational degeneracy of the equilibrium manifold in sessile droplet dynamics, which poses a key challenge for constructing solutions.
AI.method: The authors use a moving polar coordinate system with an orthogonality condition to remove degeneracy, then construct pressureless weak solutions via Galerkin method, recover pressure, obtain higher-order estimates, and apply a contraction argument.
AI.result: Global well-posedness of small perturbations of 2D sessile droplet equilibria is established, along with exponential decay estimates.
AI.conclusion: Combining local theory with previous global energy-dissipation estimates yields unique global solutions and exponential decay for the droplet system.

title: High-Order Invariant-Domain Preserving Continuous Finite Elements via Graph-Poisson Convex Limiting
summary: We develop a high-order invariant-domain preserving continuous finite element method for nonlinear scalar conservation laws. The method combines a residual-viscosity high-order discretization with a low-order invariant-domain scheme constructed on a fine $\mathbb P_1$ submesh induced by the high-order nodal points. This separation avoids the restrictions caused by nonpositive lumped masses and overly wide high-order graph stencils. The high- and low-order updates are connected by a graph-Poisson flux reconstruction, which represents their difference as conservative antisymmetric graph fluxes. These fluxes are limited using convex limiting coefficients, followed by a capacity-based mass redistribution step that restores conservation while preserving the prescribed bounds whenever sufficient admissible capacity is available. Numerical experiments for smooth and nonsmooth scalar conservation laws demonstrate high-order accuracy in smooth regimes, robustness near discontinuities, and convergence to the entropy solution for challenging benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A high-order invariant-domain preserving continuous finite element method for nonlinear scalar conservation laws using residual-viscosity discretization, fine P1 submesh, graph-Poisson flux reconstruction, convex limiting coefficients, and capacity-based mass redistribution.
AI.motivation: To overcome restrictions from nonpositive lumped masses and overly wide high-order graph stencils in invariant-domain preserving schemes for nonlinear scalar conservation laws.
AI.method: Combines residual-viscosity high-order discretization with low-order invariant-domain scheme on a fine P1 submesh; connects via graph-Poisson flux reconstruction; applies convex limiting coefficients and capacity-based mass redistribution.
AI.result: Demonstrates high-order accuracy in smooth regimes, robustness near discontinuities, and convergence to entropy solution for challenging benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively achieves high-order accuracy while preserving invariant domains and conservation properties for nonlinear scalar conservation laws.

title: A Classical Elliptic Regularity Approach to Almost Harmonic Maps and Related Systems
summary:
We establish interior regularity results for a broad class of two-dimensional nonlinear elliptic systems. Our approach isolates the core integrability mechanism within a unified abstract framework built around a Campanato-type discrete iteration scheme coupled with a Caccioppoli-type estimate. Specifically, we show that within any class of admissible pairs $(\boldsymbol{u}, \boldsymbol{f})$ that is stable under rescaling and satisfies a discrete oscillation-decay axiom, the map $\boldsymbol{u}$ is automatically locally Hölder continuous. Furthermore, the resulting Hölder exponent is explicit and optimally attains the classical Morrey--Campanato threshold dictated by the Lebesgue integrability of the source term $\boldsymbol{f}$. This purely analytic framework systematically avoids the $\mathcal{H}^1$--$\mathrm{BMO}$ duality, Wente's inequality, moving frames, and conformal uniformization techniques that underpin existing regularity theories.
  We apply this principle to derive regularity results in regimes lying strictly beyond the reach of existing gauge-theoretic methods. As a foundational example, we provide a new direct proof of local Hölder continuity for almost harmonic maps $-Δ\boldsymbol{u} = |\nabla \boldsymbol{u}|^2 \boldsymbol{u} + \boldsymbol{f}$ into $\mathbb{S}^n$ with $L^q$-integrable tension fields. We then extend the analysis to systems of the form $-Δ\boldsymbol{u} = Ω\cdot \nabla \boldsymbol{u} + \boldsymbol{f}$, replacing geometric antisymmetry assumption on the connection form $Ω\in L^2$ with the purely analytic condition $\mathrm{div} Ω\in L^q$ for some $q>1$...

AI.tldr: The paper establishes interior regularity (local Hölder continuity) for a broad class of 2D nonlinear elliptic systems using a unified Campanato-type discrete iteration framework, avoiding traditional tools like Wente's inequality, moving frames, or conformal uniformization. The method yields optimal Hölder exponents matching the Morrey-Campanato threshold.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified, purely analytic regularity theory for 2D elliptic systems that goes beyond existing gauge-theoretic methods, handling regimes (e.g., non-antisymmetric connection forms) that classical techniques cannot reach.
AI.method: A Campanato-type discrete iteration scheme combined with a Caccioppoli-type estimate, built around an oscillation-decay axiom for admissible pairs (u, f) stable under rescaling. No reliance on H¹-BMO duality, Wente's inequality, moving frames, or conformal uniformization.
AI.result: Automatic local Hölder continuity with explicit optimal exponent (Morrey-Campanato threshold). Applied to almost harmonic maps into Sⁿ with L^q tension, and to systems -Δu = Ω·∇u + f under div Ω ∈ L^q (q>1) instead of geometric antisymmetry.
AI.conclusion: The framework systematically extends regularity results beyond gauge-theoretic limits, providing a versatile analytic tool for 2D nonlinear elliptic systems.

title: When do perturbed Chebyshev--Lobatto points remain Chebyshev?
summary: Chebyshev points are distinguished in polynomial interpolation by the logarithmic growth of their Lebesgue constants. This paper asks a simple question: how much can Chebyshev points be perturbed before they cease to behave like Chebyshev points? We study perturbed Chebyshev--Lobatto nodes $x_j=\cos(jπ/n+\varepsilon_j)$, with angular perturbations $|\varepsilon_j|\leq σ_n$. The study is motivated by numerical experiments showing a broad stable region when the mesh fraction $nσ_n$ is small and rapid amplification for larger perturbations; the observed transition region is consistent with the curve $nσ_n\asymp(\log n)^{-1}$. The main result is a deterministic worst-case stability estimate: if $nσ_n(\log n+1)$ is bounded by a sufficiently small constant, then the Lebesgue constant remains logarithmic. The proof uses the cosine parametrization and Bernstein's inequality for trigonometric polynomials, thereby exploiting the angular geometry of the Chebyshev--Lobatto grid rather than a Markov inequality in the physical variable. We also give a worst-case obstruction at the angular mesh scale, showing that perturbations of order $1/n$ cannot be allowed uniformly. Consequences are derived for analytic interpolation in Bernstein ellipses, for the absence of Runge-type divergence in the stable analytic regime, and for pseudospectral differentiation. Numerical experiments illustrate the transition in the Lebesgue constants, the shape of the associated Lebesgue functions, Runge-function interpolants, and finite-precision differentiation errors.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how much Chebyshev-Lobatto nodes can be perturbed before losing their desirable logarithmic Lebesgue constant growth, establishing a stable regime when nσ_n(log n + 1) is small and showing perturbations of order 1/n are too large.
AI.motivation: Chebyshev points are renowned for optimal polynomial interpolation behavior, but the question of their robustness under perturbation—a natural practical concern—had not been systematically studied.
AI.method: The authors use the cosine parametrization to transform the problem into the angular domain, then apply Bernstein's inequality for trigonometric polynomials to derive worst-case stability bounds.
AI.result: If nσ_n(log n + 1) is bounded by a sufficiently small constant, the Lebesgue constant remains logarithmic; perturbations of order 1/n cause breakdown.
AI.conclusion: Chebyshev-Lobatto nodes are remarkably robust to small angular perturbations, with implications for analytic interpolation, Runge phenomena, and pseudospectral differentiation.

title: A sharp fixed-volume product inequality for the first $N$ nonzero Steklov eigenvalues
comment: 10 pages. Comments and suggestions are welcome
summary:
We prove a sharp fixed-volume product inequality for the first $N$ nonzero Steklov eigenvalues of bounded Lipschitz domains in $\mathbb R^N$. More precisely, if $N\ge2$ and $Ω\subset\mathbb R^N$ is a bounded Lipschitz domain, then $$
  \prod_{j=1}^N σ_j(Ω)\le \frac{ω_N}{|Ω|}, $$ where $0=σ_0(Ω)<σ_1(Ω)\leσ_2(Ω)\le\cdots$ are the Steklov eigenvalues of $Ω$, and $ω_N$ denotes the volume of the unit ball in $\mathbb R^N$. This extends the convex-domain theorem of Henrot, Philippin, and Safoui to arbitrary bounded Lipschitz domains, and in particular settles the remaining higher-dimensional case of a problem posed by Henrot.

AI.tldr: The paper proves a sharp product inequality for the first N nonzero Steklov eigenvalues of bounded Lipschitz domains in ℝ^N, extending a previous result from convex to general Lipschitz domains.
AI.motivation: To extend the convex-domain theorem of Henrot, Philippin, and Safoui to arbitrary bounded Lipschitz domains, and to settle the remaining higher-dimensional case of a problem posed by Henrot.
AI.method: The authors prove a sharp fixed-volume product inequality for the first N nonzero Steklov eigenvalues of bounded Lipschitz domains in ℝ^N (N≥2), showing that the product of these eigenvalues is bounded above by ω_N/|Ω|.
AI.result: For any bounded Lipschitz domain Ω ⊂ ℝ^N (N≥2), ∏_{j=1}^N σ_j(Ω) ≤ ω_N/|Ω|, where σ_j are the Steklov eigenvalues and ω_N is the volume of the unit ball.
AI.conclusion: The result generalizes the convex-domain theorem to all bounded Lipschitz domains and resolves the higher-dimensional case of Henrot's problem.

title: Entropy Geometry and Augmented Mobility for Reactive Maxwell--Stefan Membrane Transport with Finite Occupancy
summary:
We study a reactive Maxwell--Stefan-type membrane transport system under a finite-occupancy constraint with explicit vacancies. The admissible state space is $\mathcal{D} = \{u \in (0,1)^n : \varrho(u) := \sum_{i=1}^n u_i < 1\}$, where the vacancy fraction $1-\varrho(u)$ represents the local free volume. This bounded-occupancy geometry induces a Boltzmann--Fermi entropy and a global parametrization by entropy variables. The associated mobility is assumed to split into a composition channel, corresponding to redistribution at fixed total occupancy, and a mass channel, corresponding to variation of the filling fraction. The main structural difficulty is that the unaugmented mobility may lose coercivity in the mass channel.
  We show that a single rank-one augmentation of the form $γ_0 \mathbf{1} \otimes \mathbf{1}$ restores full coercivity while leaving the composition block unchanged. On this basis, we prove four results: a quantitative channel-wise coercivity estimate for the augmented mobility; global existence of entropy weak solutions via an implicit Rothe scheme in entropy variables; a weak--strong stability estimate in relative entropy with uniqueness in the strong class; and convergence of a fully implicit finite-volume approximation that preserves the bounded-occupancy structure and satisfies a discrete entropy inequality.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a reactive membrane transport system with bounded occupancy (explicit vacancies), showing that a rank-one augmentation restores coercivity in the mass channel, and then proves existence, stability, uniqueness, and convergence of numerical approximations.
AI.motivation: The main difficulty is that the unaugmented mobility loses coercivity in the mass channel, which hinders analysis of the PDE system. The authors aim to restore coercivity via a rank-one augmentation and establish well-posedness and numerical convergence.
AI.method: The mobility is augmented by a rank-one term γ₀ 𝟙⊗𝟙, which fixes coercivity without altering the composition block. The analysis uses: (i) quantitative channel-wise coercivity estimates; (ii) global existence via an implicit Rothe scheme in entropy variables; (iii) weak-strong stability via relative entropy; (iv) a fully implicit finite-volume scheme preserving bounded occupancy and discrete entropy inequality.
AI.result: Four results: (1) A quantitative coercivity estimate for the augmented mobility; (2) global existence of entropy weak solutions; (3) weak-strong stability with uniqueness in the strong class; (4) convergence of a fully implicit finite-volume scheme that respects the bounded-occupancy constraint and satisfies a discrete entropy inequality.
AI.conclusion: The rank-one augmentation successfully resolves the coercivity deficit, enabling a full analytical and numerical theory for this reactive membrane transport system with finite occupancy and explicit vacancies.

title: A posteriori error bounds for finite element approximations of time-dependent mean field games
summary: We present a posteriori error bounds for a general class of stabilized finite element approximations of time-dependent mean field games. We first show the equivalence between the norm of the error and the dual norm of the residual in the coupled Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman and Kolmogorov-Fokker-Planck equations. We then derive a reliable and efficient a posteriori error estimator that is based on residual estimators, along with the temporal jump estimator, and an estimator for the stabilization terms in the numerical discretization. Finally, for stabilizations based on mass-lumping in time and affine-preserving spatial stabilizations, we show that the stabilization estimators can be bounded in terms of the residual and temporal jump estimators, thus yielding an improved reliable, locally computable, and locally efficient estimator.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a posteriori error bounds for stabilized finite element approximations of time-dependent mean field games, proving equivalence between error norms and residual dual norms, and deriving reliable and efficient error estimators including residual, temporal jump, and stabilization components.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous a posteriori error control for stabilized finite element methods applied to time-dependent mean field games, which are important for applications in economics and finance but lack such error analysis.
AI.method: The authors prove norm equivalence between the error and the dual norm of the residual for the coupled HJB-KFP system, then construct an a posteriori error estimator comprising residual estimators, temporal jump estimators, and stabilization estimators, and finally show that for specific stabilizations (mass-lumping in time and affine-preserving in space), the stabilization estimators are bounded by the other components.
AI.result: A reliable and efficient a posteriori error estimator is derived; for mass-lumping and affine-preserving stabilizations, an improved estimator is obtained that is reliable, locally computable, and locally efficient.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides computable and efficient error bounds for stabilized FEM solutions of time-dependent mean field games, with theoretical guarantees on reliability and efficiency.

title: Polar director structure of SmAP$_\text{F}$ phase of bent-core liquid crystals in thin planar cells with bias electric field
summary: We study the polar director structure in thin planar cells filled with bent-core liquid crystals in the ferroelectric smectic-A phase (SmAP$_\text{F}$). We analyze a continuum phenomenological model proposed in the physics literature and present rigorous proofs of the existence and uniqueness of the equilibrium solutions. We further investigate the qualitative properties of nontrivial solutions and examine the effects of a bias electric field, surface anchoring, and cell thickness on the polar director configuration. Our results are consistent with previous experimental and numerical simulations reported in the physics literature. In addition, our analysis reveals new parameter-dependent behaviors supported by our numerical simulations and extends results reported from previous literature.

AI.tldr: This paper provides rigorous mathematical proofs for the existence, uniqueness, and qualitative properties of equilibrium solutions in the polar director structure of bent-core liquid crystals in the ferroelectric smectic-A phase (SmAP_F), validating and extending previous experimental and numerical findings.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous mathematical proofs supporting a continuum phenomenological model for polar director structures in bent-core liquid crystals in the SmAP_F phase, which had previously only been studied through experiments and numerical simulations.
AI.method: The authors analyze a continuum phenomenological model from physics literature, develop rigorous proofs of existence and uniqueness of equilibrium solutions, and investigate qualitative properties of nontrivial solutions under varying conditions (bias electric field, surface anchoring, cell thickness). Numerical simulations are also employed to reveal new parameter-dependent behaviors.
AI.result: Results are consistent with previous experimental and numerical simulations. Additionally, new parameter-dependent behaviors are discovered through combined analytical and numerical approaches, extending the findings from prior literature.
AI.conclusion: The rigorous mathematical analysis validates the phenomenological model and reveals new insights into how bias electric field, surface anchoring, and cell thickness affect polar director configurations in SmAP_F phase bent-core liquid crystals.

title: Multiword Arithmetic and Parallel Computing
comment: Presented at the 12-th Ada Developer Room at FOSDEM 2025
summary: In many applications, the precision by the available hardware arithmetic is insufficient to guarantee accurate results. Multiword arithmetic is a special type of multiprecision arithmetic where a multiple double is an unevaluated sum of 64-bit doubles, or where a multiple integer is an unevaluated sum of 64-bit integers. Parallel computing is applied to compensate for the cost overhead of multiword arithmetic. This type of arithmetic exploits naturally the optimized hardware, allows for efficient type conversions, memory layouts, all favorable for parallel computing. For example, storing a multiword in registers rather than arrays is beneficial to parallel computing by tasking and acceleration by graphics processing units. Code for multiword arithmetic is available in the software PHCpack, written mainly in Ada, publicly available at github, and as an Alire crate, released under the GNU GPL v3.0 license.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses multiword arithmetic (using unevaluated sums of 64-bit doubles/integers) to overcome hardware precision limitations, leveraging parallel computing to offset overhead. The implementation is available in PHCpack (Ada, GitHub, Alire crate, GPL v3.0).
AI.motivation: Hardware arithmetic precision is often insufficient for accurate results in many applications, motivating the need for multiword arithmetic.
AI.method: Multiword arithmetic represents numbers as unevaluated sums of 64-bit doubles (multiple double) or 64-bit integers (multiple integer), and uses parallel computing (e.g., tasking, GPUs) to compensate for the computational overhead.
AI.result: Efficient type conversions and memory layouts are achieved; storing multiword in registers rather than arrays benefits parallel tasking and GPU acceleration. Code is available in PHCpack (Ada, GitHub, Alire crate).
AI.conclusion: Multiword arithmetic leverages optimized hardware and parallel computing to improve precision, with practical open-source implementation available.

title: Normalized solutions for a nonlinear Dirac equation with an inhomogeneous nonlinearity

AI.tldr: This paper proves existence of normalized solutions to the nonlinear Dirac equation with a singular potential |x|^{-b} for all sufficiently small masses a>0, covering subcritical, critical, and supercritical nonlinearities, using a constrained min-max reduction method and a novel weighted compact embedding.
AI.motivation: To establish the existence of normalized solutions for the nonlinear Dirac equation with a singular weight |x|^{-b}, which poses challenges due to the strongly indefinite Dirac operator and loss of translation invariance. The goal is a unified theory valid for small masses across all nonlinearity regimes.
AI.method: Combines a constrained min-max reduction method with a novel weighted compact embedding in L^p(R^3,|x|^{-b} dx; C^4) to handle the singular potential at the origin and the indefinite nature of the Dirac operator.
AI.result: For any b∈(0,1) and p∈(2,3-b), a normalized solution exists for all sufficiently small masses a>0, with μ∈(0,m) and u∈H^{1/2}(R^3;C^4). This covers mass-subcritical, mass-critical, and mass-supercritical cases.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully overcomes the dual difficulties of strong indefiniteness and loss of translation invariance, providing a unified existence result in the small-mass regime for the full range of nonlinearities.

authors: J. J. W. van der Vegt
title: Kreiss stability analysis of Hagstrom-Warburton nonreflecting boundary conditions for the first-order time-dependent Maxwell equations
summary: We analyze the stability of the Hagstrom-Warburton nonreflecting boundary conditions (HW-NRBCs) for the first-order time-dependent Maxwell equations. The HW-NRBCs enjoy very small reflection coefficients and do not use high-order derivatives or nonlocal boundary operators, which makes them well-suited for high-order accurate numerical discretizations. The main result of this paper is an $L^2$ a-priori bound on the solution in terms of initial and boundary data and volume sources in a half-space. To obtain this result, we first derive a mapping that takes outgoing components to ingoing components of the solution of the Maxwell equations with HW-NRBCs, which shows that the Kreiss condition does not hold uniformly. Next, to prove stability, several symmetrizers are constructed, which establishes well-posedness in a generalized sense.

AI.tldr: This paper proves $L^2$ stability of Hagstrom-Warburton nonreflecting boundary conditions for the first-order time-dependent Maxwell equations by constructing symmetrizers, despite the Kreiss condition failing uniformly.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous stability guarantees for HW-NRBCs, which are attractive for high-order numerical discretizations due to small reflection coefficients and avoidance of high-order derivatives or nonlocal operators.
AI.method: Derives a mapping from outgoing to ingoing components showing the Kreiss condition does not hold uniformly, then constructs several symmetrizers to prove well-posedness in a generalized sense.
AI.result: An $L^2$ a-priori bound on the solution in terms of initial/boundary data and volume sources in a half-space is established.
AI.conclusion: HW-NRBCs are stable for Maxwell equations despite the non-uniform Kreiss condition, validated via symmetrizer-based well-posedness analysis.

title: An Asynchronous multi-rate Taylor method for Delay Differential Equations
summary:
The numerical simulation of high-dimensional, multi-rate Delay
  Differential Equations (DDEs) is fundamentally bottlenecked by
  synchronous time-stepping and the dynamic memory allocation required
  for continuous history tracking. In this paper, we introduce the
  Asynchronous Adaptive Taylor Solver (AATS), an event-driven
  integration framework designed to overcome these high-performance
  computing limitations. By assigning independent local clocks to
  individual coordinates and advancing them using high-order Taylor
  polynomials generated via compile-time Automatic Differentiation, AATS
  restricts computational work to actively evolving sub-graphs. To
  eliminate the severe memory overhead endemic to traditional DDE
  solvers, AATS utilizes statically allocated circular buffers to store
  polynomial segments, achieving interpolation-free continuous
  dense-output evaluation with a verified zero-allocation runtime memory
  footprint.
  Alongside this software architecture, we establish a novel continuous
  proof of convergence for asynchronous Taylor expansions and formally
  prove that the framework's algorithmic complexity scales linearly
  (O(N)). Extensive benchmarks against state-of-the-art synchronous
  solvers (Julia SciML) validate these theoretical bounds. On
  large-scale benchmarks (upto $N = 10000$ coordinates) AATS
  fundamentally minimizes the constant factor of algorithmic work by
  avoiding redundant evaluations, delivering empirically consistent with
  O(N) execution scaling and significant wall-clock speedups.

AI.tldr: AATS is an event-driven, asynchronous solver for high-dimensional DDEs that uses local clocks, compile-time AD, and circular buffers to achieve O(N) scaling, zero-allocation runtime, and significant speedups over synchronous solvers.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional, multi-rate DDE simulation is bottlenecked by synchronous time-stepping and dynamic memory allocation for history tracking, limiting HPC performance.
AI.method: AATS assigns independent local clocks per coordinate, uses high-order Taylor polynomials via compile-time Automatic Differentiation, and stores polynomial segments in statically allocated circular buffers for zero-allocation dense output.
AI.result: Formal proof of convergence for asynchronous Taylor expansions; proven O(N) algorithmic complexity; benchmarks on up to N=10000 coordinates show O(N) scaling and significant wall-clock speedups vs. Julia SciML solvers.
AI.conclusion: AATS fundamentally overcomes DDE simulation bottlenecks by minimizing redundant work and memory overhead, delivering empirically validated linear scaling and practical speedups.

title: Finite difference methods for a continuous-time heterogeneous agent model with recursive utility
summary: We propose, analyze and test computational methods for solving a continuous-time heterogenous agent model with Epstein-Zin utility. Such recursive utilities allow the model to disentangle between risk aversion and intertemporal substitution. Having discretized the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation arising in the model, we propose a Howard-Newton algorithm for the late resolution preference case, and a Howard-Tarski-Kantorovich algorithm for the early resolution preference case. We prove the convergence of the iterative algorithms. We obtain as a consequence the existence of solutions to the discretized HJB equations. In the late resolution case, we supply a priori estimates between the unique solutions of the continuous and discretized HJB equations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes and proves convergence of Howard-Newton and Howard-Tarski-Kantorovich algorithms for solving discretized HJB equations in a continuous-time heterogeneous agent model with Epstein-Zin utility, handling both late and early resolution preferences.
AI.motivation: To solve continuous-time heterogeneous agent models with Epstein-Zin utility, which allows disentangling risk aversion from intertemporal substitution, by developing provably convergent computational methods for the resulting HJB equations.
AI.method: Discretizes the HJB equation from the model, then proposes a Howard-Newton algorithm for late resolution preference and a Howard-Tarski-Kantorovich algorithm for early resolution preference, with convergence proofs and a priori error estimates.
AI.result: Proves convergence of both iterative algorithms, establishes existence of solutions to discretized HJB equations, and provides a priori error estimates between continuous and discretized solutions for the late resolution case.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithms are theoretically sound for solving this class of models, with guaranteed convergence and error bounds, enabling reliable numerical analysis of heterogeneous agent economies with recursive preferences.

title: Existence of optimizers for the sharp stability constant in the logarithmic Sobolev inequality
summary: We study the sharp stability constant in the logarithmic Sobolev inequality, defined as the infimum of the logarithmic Sobolev deficit divided by the squared $L^2$-distance from the manifold of Gaussian optimizers. For every $N\geq 1$, first we prove that this infimum is attained in the Euclidean formulation of the inequality. Then, we show that every such optimizer decays exponentially fast at infinity, and therefore gives rise to an optimizer in the Gaussian formulation, where the same sharp constant appears. The proof is based on an extension of the general strategy introduced by Bianchi and Egnell for the Sobolev inequality and recently developed by König to prove the existence of optimizers for the corresponding sharp stability constant. The main difference in the logarithmic Sobolev setting is the analysis of minimizing sequences approaching the manifold of optimizers, since the entropy term $u^2\log u^2$ does not allow for a direct global second-order expansion. This is overcome by a perturbative estimate near the Gaussian manifold, which provides the compactness threshold needed to rule out the loss of compactness.

AI.tldr: This paper proves existence of optimizers for the sharp stability constant in the logarithmic Sobolev inequality, using a Bianchi-Egnell type strategy with novel handling of the entropy term's non-quadratic behavior near Gaussians.
AI.motivation: To establish the existence of optimizers for the sharp stability constant in logarithmic Sobolev inequality, extending the Bianchi-Egnell framework which previously worked for Sobolev inequalities.
AI.method: Extends the Bianchi-Egnell strategy (via König) to the logarithmic Sobolev setting. Overcomes the challenge that the entropy term u²log u² prevents direct global second-order expansion by using a perturbative estimate near the Gaussian manifold to establish compactness threshold.
AI.result: Proves the infimum defining the sharp stability constant is attained in the Euclidean formulation for every N≥1, and shows optimizers decay exponentially fast, yielding optimizers also in the Gaussian formulation.
AI.conclusion: The sharp stability constant for logarithmic Sobolev inequality has optimizers, proven via a refined compactness argument that handles the non-quadratic entropy term through near-Gaussian perturbative estimates.

title: Solve for the Hyperparameter, Skip the Search: Kolmogorov-Optimal Scaling Laws for Spline Regression
summary: Hyperparameter tuning almost always means search: fit the model at every value on a grid, score each by cross-validation, and keep the winner. For spline regression that search is unnecessary. The optimal resolution can be solved for in closed form, to the accuracy an exhaustive search reaches, at a fraction of the compute. Three ingredients make this possible: classical approximation theory pins the squared bias to a known power of the resolution G, exactly the Kolmogorov n-width of the smoothness class; the basis dimension is an explicit polynomial in G; and leave-one-out error follows from a single fit via the PRESS identity. Balancing the two known curves gives the minimizer analytically. We extend this calculus to many coordinates by replacing ambient input dimension with interaction order, the number of active low-order components in an ANOVA decomposition, yielding a scaling law in which the optimal resolution and error are power functions of the effective density (sample size per active component), with input dimension absent from the exponent. The law becomes an algorithm. KORE (Kolmogorov-optimal Order-aware Resolution Estimation) fits two pilot resolutions, solves a leverage-calibrated 2x2 system for the bias and noise scales, and evaluates the closed-form plug-in resolution with a tiny leave-one-out certificate: about a dozen fits instead of a full grid sweep, with a consistency guarantee as the sample grows. Across additive and sparse pairwise targets up to 80 input dimensions, KORE matches exhaustive 3-fold cross-validation and the full classical ladder (GCV, Mallows' Cp, AIC, BIC) while fitting roughly 8x fewer models; on 36 real tabular datasets it ranks first among 21 methods in accuracy per unit of compute, ahead of tuned boosters and kernel machines. When complexity lives in low interaction order, solving for the resolution beats searching for it.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces KORE, a closed-form method for optimal spline resolution tuning that eliminates grid search by leveraging classical approximation theory and the PRESS identity, achieving comparable or better accuracy with ~8x fewer model fits than exhaustive cross-validation across diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: Hyperparameter tuning for spline regression typically requires expensive grid search and cross-validation. The authors aim to find a closed-form solution for optimal resolution that avoids this computational burden.
AI.method: KORE combines three key ideas: (1) classical approximation theory linking squared bias to a known power of resolution G (Kolmogorov n-width), (2) basis dimension as an explicit polynomial in G, and (3) leave-one-out error via PRESS identity. It fits two pilot resolutions to solve a 2x2 system for bias/noise scales, then computes the optimal resolution analytically with a leave-one-out certificate. For high dimensions, it replaces input dimension with interaction order in an ANOVA decomposition.
AI.result: KORE matches exhaustive 3-fold CV and classical criteria (GCV, Cp, AIC, BIC) while fitting ~8x fewer models. It works up to 80 input dimensions for additive and sparse pairwise targets. On 36 real tabular datasets, it ranks first among 21 methods in accuracy per compute, ahead of tuned boosters and kernel machines.
AI.conclusion: When complexity lives in low interaction order, solving for the resolution beats searching for it. The closed-form approach yields a scaling law where optimal resolution and error are power functions of effective density independent of input dimension.

title: The fractional Porous Medium Equation on graphs
summary: We study the fractional porous medium equation on connected infinite graphs with no local finiteness assumption. We introduce a notion of weak dual solution adapted to the discrete setting, and establish existence results for nonnegative initial data belonging to a weighted space defined through the fractional Green function, extending beyond the classical $\ell^1$ framework. Our approach relies on weighted estimates and on a detailed analysis of the associated fractional Green function. In the particular case of infinite trees with standard weights, we establish comparison principles and derive estimates for the fractional Green function, which lead to quantitative smoothing effects for solutions.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the study of the fractional porous medium equation to infinite graphs without local finiteness, introducing weak dual solutions and establishing existence results for nonnegative initial data in weighted spaces beyond ℓ¹, with quantitative smoothing effects proven for infinite trees.
AI.motivation: To extend the fractional porous medium equation to general infinite graphs (without local finiteness), and to establish existence and regularity results beyond classical ℓ¹ settings using weighted spaces.
AI.method: Introduces a discrete notion of weak dual solution; uses weighted estimates and detailed analysis of the fractional Green function; for infinite trees, applies comparison principles and Green function estimates.
AI.result: Existence results for nonnegative initial data in weighted spaces defined via the fractional Green function; for infinite trees, quantitative smoothing effects for solutions are derived.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully generalizes the fractional porous medium equation to non-locally-finite graphs, with concrete smoothing estimates on trees.

title: Robust Structure Learning of $k$-local Lindbladians
summary: We present an efficient protocol for learning an unknown $k$-local Lindblad generator on $n$ qubits using only product-state preparations, short-time evolution, and single-qubit Pauli measurements, without prior knowledge of the interaction structure. For fixed $k$ and bounded weighted interaction strength, the protocol estimates all Hamiltonian and dissipative Pauli--GKSL coefficients to entrywise accuracy $\varepsilon$ with probability at least $1-δ$ using $\widetilde{\mathcal O}_k(\varepsilon^{-2}n^{2k}\log(1/δ))$ samples and polylogarithmically many evolution times. A semidefinite projection converts these estimates into a valid $k$-local Lindblad generator with diamond-norm error at most $\varepsilon$ using $\widetilde{\mathcal O}_k(\varepsilon^{-2}n^{4k}\log(1/δ))$ samples and polynomial-time classical postprocessing. If a suitable set of influential coefficients is supplied and satisfies a stable sparsity condition, the dependence on $n$ can improve from polynomial to logarithmic; in particular, exact supports of bounded intersection degree require only $\widetilde{\mathcal O}_k(\varepsilon^{-2}\log(n/δ))$ samples, with analogous reductions in system-size dependence for sufficiently decaying long-range interactions. We also provide a robust structure-learning procedure, extend the guarantees to model misspecification, and prove complementary sample-complexity lower bounds. To our knowledge, these are the first efficient learning guarantees for general $k$-local dissipative quantum dynamics under such limited experimental control.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an efficient protocol for learning unknown k-local Lindblad generators on n qubits using simple experimental setups (product states, short-time evolution, single-qubit measurements). It achieves sample-efficient estimation of Hamiltonian and dissipative coefficients, with improvements under sparsity conditions, and provides the first efficient learning guarantees for general k-local dissipative quantum dynamics under limited experimental control.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient, experimentally feasible protocol for learning unknown k-local open quantum systems (Lindblad generators) without requiring prior knowledge of interaction structure, complex state preparations, or long-time evolution, which has been an open challenge.
AI.method: The protocol uses product-state preparations, short-time evolution, and single-qubit Pauli measurements to estimate all Hamiltonian and dissipative Pauli-GKSL coefficients entrywise. A semidefinite projection converts noisy estimates into a valid k-local Lindblad generator. The approach is extended to handle stable sparsity conditions for improved scaling, robust structure learning, and model misspecification.
AI.result: Achieves Õ_k(ε^{-2}n^{2k}log(1/δ)) samples for entrywise estimation and Õ_k(ε^{-2}n^{4k}log(1/δ)) for diamond-norm error. Under stable sparsity (e.g., bounded intersection degree), sample complexity reduces to Õ_k(ε^{-2}log(n/δ)). Complementary lower bounds are also provided.
AI.conclusion: This work presents the first efficient learning guarantees for general k-local dissipative quantum dynamics under limited experimental control, with polynomial-to-logarithmic improvements under sparsity, and extensions to structure learning and model misspecification.

title: Strong traces for solutions of $2\times 2$ nonlinear hyperbolic systems
summary: In this paper we show that bounded entropy solutions of genuinely nonlinear $2\times 2$ systems of hyperbolic conservation laws admit a strong trace on Lipschitz curves. The main argument relies on a new half-space Liouville-type theorem for isentropic solutions with constant normal traces, providing the first extension to general genuinely nonlinear systems of the strong trace properties available for scalar conservation laws.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that bounded entropy solutions for genuinely nonlinear 2×2 hyperbolic conservation laws have strong traces on Lipschitz curves, using a new half-space Liouville-type theorem for isentropic solutions with constant normal traces.
AI.motivation: To extend strong trace properties, previously available only for scalar conservation laws, to general genuinely nonlinear 2×2 systems of hyperbolic conservation laws.
AI.method: The main argument is based on a new half-space Liouville-type theorem for isentropic solutions with constant normal traces.
AI.result: Bounded entropy solutions of genuinely nonlinear 2×2 systems admit a strong trace on Lipschitz curves.
AI.conclusion: This provides the first extension of strong trace properties from scalar conservation laws to general genuinely nonlinear systems.

title: Formation of singularities for a family of one-dimensional quasilinear wave equations beyond the variational case
summary:
We consider finite-time singularity formation for classical solutions to the following parameterized nonlinear wave equation: \[
  u_{tt}=c(u)^2u_{xx}+λc(u)c'(u)(u_x)^2, \] where \(λ\in[0,2]\) is a parameter. In previous works, it is known that finite-time blow-up solutions exist for \(0<λ\le1\) and for \(λ=2\).
  The cases \(λ=1\) and \(λ=2\) are known as the variational wave equation and the \(p\)-system, respectively, and they possess symmetric structures or conservation laws. For \(0<λ<1\), the blow-up construction is based on the fact that, after decomposing the wave into two Riemann variables, one component can be kept sufficiently small and hence does not prevent the other component from blowing up. In the present paper, we treat the remaining intermediate case \(1<λ<2\). This case is essentially different from the previous one, since both Riemann variables may grow.
  The key idea is to introduce suitable supremum functions for the Riemann variables and to derive a comparison principle through right Dini derivatives. This allows us to control the relative size of the two components and to obtain a Riccati-type differential inequality for the dominant supremum function.

AI.tldr: This paper proves finite-time blow-up for classical solutions of a parameterized nonlinear wave equation in the intermediate parameter range 1<λ<2, using supremum functions and a comparison principle via Dini derivatives to control both growing Riemann variables.
AI.motivation: Previous works established blow-up for 0<λ≤1 and λ=2, but the intermediate case 1<λ<2 remained open because both Riemann variables can grow simultaneously, unlike the previous cases where one component stays small.
AI.method: Introduce suitable supremum functions for the two Riemann variables and derive a comparison principle through right Dini derivatives, leading to a Riccati-type differential inequality for the dominant supremum function.
AI.result: Finite-time singularity formation is established for 1<λ<2, completing the full parameter range analysis.
AI.conclusion: The paper fills the gap in the parameter space (1<λ<2) by a novel comparison technique, showing blow-up occurs even when both wave components grow, unlike previous constructions that relied on one component remaining small.

title: Krein's Formula for Conic Laplacians on Compact Riemann Surfaces
summary: In this paper, we establish Krein's formula for self-adjoint extensions of conic Laplacians on compact Riemann surfaces. Our approach is based on the finite-dimensional symplectic space of critical asymptotic boundary data: self-adjoint extensions are parametrized by Lagrangian subspaces, and the resolvent difference of two extensions is expressed in terms of the associated Weyl function. We further derive a trace identity for the resolvent difference and use it to prove a comparison formula for the positive-spectrum zeta determinants associated with different Lagrangian boundary conditions, including the contributions of negative eigenvalues and zero modes.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes Krein's formula for self-adjoint extensions of conic Laplacians on compact Riemann surfaces, using Lagrangian subspaces to parametrize extensions and deriving trace identities and zeta determinant comparison formulas.
AI.motivation: To extend Krein's formula to conic Laplacians on compact Riemann surfaces, enabling a rigorous treatment of self-adjoint extensions and spectral properties in singular geometric settings.
AI.method: Uses finite-dimensional symplectic space of critical asymptotic boundary data, parametrizes self-adjoint extensions by Lagrangian subspaces, expresses resolvent differences via Weyl functions, and derives trace identities for comparison of zeta determinants.
AI.result: Establishes Krein's formula for conic Laplacians, derives a trace identity for resolvent differences, and proves a comparison formula for positive-spectrum zeta determinants under different Lagrangian boundary conditions, accounting for negative eigenvalues and zero modes.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a complete spectral comparison for conic Laplacians with different boundary conditions, generalizing Krein's theory to singular Riemann surfaces.

title: Finite-Horizon Portfolio Choice, Labor Supply, and Early Retirement under Borrowing Constraints

AI.tldr: This paper solves an optimal consumption and portfolio problem with flexible labor supply and an irreversible early retirement option under borrowing constraints, using dual martingale methods and variational inequalities.
AI.motivation: To understand how labor supply flexibility affects optimal consumption, investment, and retirement timing decisions when agents face borrowing constraints and irreversible retirement.
AI.method: Dual martingale method transforms the primal problem into a zero-sum stopper-singular-controller game; the dual value is characterized by a min-max parabolic variational inequality with obstacle and gradient constraints.
AI.result: The maximal strong solution of the variational inequality is unique and admissible, with a monotone-increasing gradient-constrained free boundary (binding boundary). Verification identifies this solution with the game value, and duality recovers optimal policies.
AI.conclusion: Numerical analysis illustrates how labor supply flexibility influences consumption, portfolio choice, and retirement timing under borrowing constraints.

title: Global Extrema of the Zeta Regularized Determinant on Orthogonal Flat Tori
summary: The search for extremal geometries is a central theme in several areas of mathematics. Here, we address the following question: among all n-dimensional orthogonal tori of unit volume, which one maximizes the zeta regularized determinant of the Laplacian? We prove that the equilateral torus is the unique maximizer in each dimension n, for all n greater than or equal to 2, validating Sarnak's conjecture in this context. We also investigate the analogous question for the Laplacian on Euclidean boxes with the Neumann and Dirichlet boundary conditions. For orthogonal flat tori of unit volume and dimension n, we show further that the determinant is strictly decreasing with the dimension and tends to zero as the dimension n tends to infinity.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that among all n-dimensional orthogonal tori of unit volume (n≥2), the equilateral torus uniquely maximizes the zeta-regularized determinant of the Laplacian, confirming Sarnak's conjecture. It also studies analogous problems for Euclidean boxes with Neumann/Dirichlet boundary conditions and shows the determinant strictly decreases with dimension and tends to zero as n→∞.
AI.motivation: To identify which orthogonal tori of unit volume maximize the zeta-regularized determinant of the Laplacian, addressing Sarnak's conjecture and extending the search for extremal geometries across different settings.
AI.method: The authors use analytic and spectral methods to compare the zeta-regularized determinants across different orthogonal tori (and Euclidean boxes), likely involving comparisons of eigenvalues and zeta functions parameterized by side lengths.
AI.result: The equilateral torus is the unique maximizer for all n≥2. For orthogonal flat tori, the determinant strictly decreases with dimension and tends to zero as n→∞.
AI.conclusion: The equilateral torus is optimal for maximizing the determinant in each dimension, confirming Sarnak's conjecture; the determinant's behavior also has monotonic and limiting properties in high dimensions.

title: Spectral Analysis for Finite-Time Singularities of Lagrangian Mean Curvature Flow
summary: Let $\mathcal C$ be a $G$-invariant special Lagrangian cone admitting a scaled family of $G$-invariant special Lagrangian desingularizations $a \overline L$ which converge to $\mathcal C$ as $a\searrow 0$. We study the linearized self-shrinker operator on $a\overline L$ in a Gaussian weighted $L^2$ space of $G$-equivariant functions. For $0<a\ll1$, we construct any prescribed finite number of eigenfunctions whose eigenvalues converge to those of the limiting conical operator, and we prove a spectral gap estimate on the orthogonal complement of these modes. We also identify the lowest eigenfunction with the scaling mode of the special Lagrangian desingularization. This spectral basis provides the analytic foundation for the construction of Type II blow-up solutions of Lagrangian mean curvature flow in the companion paper.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the linearized self-shrinker operator on G-invariant special Lagrangian desingularizations, constructing eigenfunctions with prescribed eigenvalues and proving spectral gap estimates to provide analytic foundations for Type II blow-up solutions in Lagrangian mean curvature flow.
AI.motivation: To establish the spectral theory needed for constructing Type II blow-up solutions of Lagrangian mean curvature flow, specifically by understanding the linearized self-shrinker operator on desingularized special Lagrangian cones.
AI.method: The authors analyze the linearized self-shrinker operator on a scaled family of G-invariant special Lagrangian desingularizations using Gaussian weighted L² spaces of G-equivariant functions. They construct eigenfunctions whose eigenvalues converge to those of the limiting conical operator and prove spectral gap estimates on orthogonal complements.
AI.result: Construction of any prescribed finite number of eigenfunctions with eigenvalues converging to the limiting conical operator's eigenvalues; a spectral gap estimate on the orthogonal complement of these modes; identification of the lowest eigenfunction as the scaling mode of the desingularization.
AI.conclusion: The spectral basis developed provides the necessary analytic foundation for constructing Type II blow-up solutions of Lagrangian mean curvature flow in the companion paper.

title: Mean field limit of non-exchangeable interacting diffusions on co-evolutionary networks
summary: Systems in which the network structure and particle states co-evolve in mutual influence are increasingly recognized as essential for modeling complex adaptive systems. However, traditional models of non-exchangeable interacting particle systems frequently assume a fixed network topology, a simplification that fails to capture the dynamical nature of many real-world phenomena. In this paper, we rigorously establish the mean-field limit for systems of non-exchangeable interacting diffusions on co-evolutionary networks. The primary analytical challenge arises from the coupling between the network dynamics and the agents' states, which induces non-Markovian dynamics where the system's evolution depends on its entire history. Consequently, the macroscopic limit is not governed by a classical partial differential equation, but rather by a coupled system of path-dependent McKean-Vlasov SDEs for the particles' states together with a transport equation for the distribution of the weights. A further difficulty stems from the non-linear weight dynamics, which requires an adequate choice for the limiting network structure. To overcome the structural limitations of classical graphon theory, we employ the framework of \(\mathcal{K}\)-graphons (Lovász and Szegedy, 2010), also termed probability-graphons (Abraham, Delmas, and Weibel, 2025). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example in the literature in which the natural topology of probability-graphons is used in the context of mean-field limits, providing a natural and rigorous framework that is fully compatible with non-linear network adaptivity.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes the mean-field limit for systems of non-exchangeable interacting diffusions on co-evolutionary networks using probability-graphons, addressing the challenge of non-Markovian dynamics induced by coupled network-particle evolution.
AI.motivation: Traditional models of interacting particle systems assume fixed network topologies, which fails to capture real-world complex adaptive systems where network structure and particle states co-evolve in mutual influence.
AI.method: The authors employ the framework of $\mathcal{K}$-graphons (probability-graphons) to overcome structural limitations of classical graphon theory, and derive a macroscopic limit governed by path-dependent McKean-Vlasov SDEs coupled with a transport equation for weight distributions.
AI.result: The mean-field limit is rigorously established, yielding not a classical PDE but a coupled system of path-dependent McKean-Vlasov SDEs and a transport equation. This is the first use of probability-graphon topology in mean-field limits.
AI.conclusion: The probability-graphon framework provides a natural and rigorous approach fully compatible with non-linear network adaptivity, offering a foundation for modeling complex adaptive systems with co-evolving structure and dynamics.

title: Parabolic rectifiability of the Brakke flow
summary:
We prove that the support of the canonical space-time measure for a Brakke flow is a parabolic $(k+2)$-rectifiable set. As a consequence, we obtain that at almost all points along the flow, with respect to this canonical space-time measure, there exists a unique, static, planar tangent flow, and that various notions of density for the flow agree at these points.
  Moreover, following on from our previous work `The space-time-Grassmann measure of the Brakke flow', we continue to develop the approach to the Brakke flow as a space-time-Grassmann measure. We prove that the standard notion of convergence for Brakke flows, coming from the compactness theorem of Ilmanen (7.1 of `Elliptic regularization and partial regularity for motion by mean curvature'), is equivalent to the convergence of these space-time-Grassmann Radon measures. This gives an alternate notion of varifold convergence to the one exhibited in 7.1(ii) of `Elliptic regularization and partial regularity for motion by mean curvature'.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that the support of the canonical space-time measure for Brakke flow is parabolically (k+2)-rectifiable, implying unique static planar tangent flows and density agreement almost everywhere. It also establishes equivalence between standard Brakke flow convergence and space-time-Grassmann Radon measure convergence.
AI.motivation: To develop a refined understanding of the structure of Brakke flows at the level of space-time measures, and to establish a connection between the standard Ilmanen compactness framework and a Grassmannian measure-based approach.
AI.method: Geometric measure theory techniques, combining rectifiability analysis of space-time measures with the space-time-Grassmann measure formalism previously developed by the authors.
AI.result: (1) The canonical space-time measure support is parabolically (k+2)-rectifiable. (2) Almost everywhere there exists a unique static planar tangent flow. (3) Various density notions coincide at typical points. (4) Ilmanen's standard convergence is equivalent to space-time-Grassmann Radon measure convergence.
AI.conclusion: The space-time-Grassmann measure approach provides an equivalent but potentially more refined framework for studying Brakke flow convergence and regularity.

title: The Fractal Neural Operator: Overcoming Spectral Bias in Chaotic Attractors via Prime-Harmonic Weierstrass Encodings
summary: Deep learning models, particularly Transformers and Neural Operators, exhibit a well-documented "spectral bias," effectively acting as low-pass filters that smooth out high-frequency information. While benign in fluid dynamics, this bias is catastrophic for Chaotic Dynamical Systems, where the underlying strange attractor is characterized by fractal geometry and infinite spectral density. We introduce the Fractal Neural Operator (FNO), a novel architecture that utilizes a non-resonant prime number basis to approximate continuous dynamical systems. Unlike geometric encodings ($2^k$), which suffer from spectral gaps and resonance, our Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder injects infinite spectral resolution into the latent space. We demonstrate that FNO extends the valid prediction horizon of the Lorenz-63 system to 347 Lyapunov times, exceeding state-of-the-art Reservoir Computing baselines by a factor of 2.3x. These results suggest that "chaos" is not inherently unpredictable to neural networks, but rather requires non-differentiable, fractal embedding manifolds.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Fractal Neural Operator (FNO), which uses a non-resonant prime number basis and Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder to overcome spectral bias in deep learning models, extending chaos prediction in Lorenz-63 to 347 Lyapunov times—2.3x better than state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models exhibit spectral bias (low-pass filtering), which is catastrophic for chaotic dynamical systems characterized by fractal geometry and infinite spectral density. Existing architectures fail to accurately predict chaotic systems over long horizons.
AI.method: FNO uses a non-resonant prime number basis and a Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder to inject infinite spectral resolution into the latent space, avoiding spectral gaps and resonance issues found in geometric encodings ($2^k$).
AI.result: FNO achieves prediction horizons of 347 Lyapunov times on Lorenz-63, exceeding Reservoir Computing baselines by 2.3x.
AI.conclusion: Chaos is not inherently unpredictable to neural networks; rather, it requires non-differentiable, fractal embedding manifolds to overcome spectral bias.

title: Compactness and Its Applications of Sobolev Spaces Associated with Ball Banach Function Spaces
summary: Let $N\in\mathbb{N}\cap[2,\infty),$ $Ω$ be a bounded Lipschitz domain in $\mathbb{R}^N$, and $X(Ω)$ be a ball Banach function space on $Ω.$ In this article, under some mild assumptions, we establish a compactness theorem for Sobolev spaces associated with $X(Ω)$. Different from the fractional Sobolev space, our proof is based on an elaborate decomposition of bounded Lipschitz domains and its corresponding weighted fractional Poincaré inequality on each piece. As applications, we obtain the fractional Poincaré inequality in $X(Ω)$ that, for any $s\in (s_0,1)$ and $f\in X(Ω)$, \begin{align*} \|f-f_Ω\|_{X(Ω)} \lesssim(1-s)^{\frac 1q} \left\|\left[\int_Ω\frac{|f(\cdot)-f(y)|^q}{|\cdot-y|^{N+sq}}\,dy \right]^{\frac{1}{q}} \right\|_{X(Ω)}, \end{align*} where $s_0$ is a given positive constant and the implicit positive constant is independent of $s$ and $f$. Using this, we further establish the well-posedness of a weighted Triebel--Lizorkin type nonlocal variational problem. These results are of wide generality and, even when they are applied to Morrey spaces, weighted Lebesgue spaces, mixed-norm Lebesgue spaces, variable Lebesgue spaces, Orlicz spaces, and Orlicz-slice spaces, the obtained results are also new.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a compactness theorem for Sobolev spaces associated with ball Banach function spaces on bounded Lipschitz domains, using domain decomposition and weighted fractional Poincaré inequalities. It then proves a fractional Poincaré inequality and applies it to the well-posedness of a weighted Triebel-Lizorkin type nonlocal variational problem, with results that are new even for many specific function spaces.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified framework for compactness results in Sobolev spaces associated with general ball Banach function spaces, going beyond classical or fractional settings, and to derive fractional Poincaré inequalities that work across many function space types.
AI.method: The proof uses an elaborate decomposition of bounded Lipschitz domains into pieces and applies a weighted fractional Poincaré inequality on each piece. This avoids the fractional Sobolev space approach. The resulting inequality is then used to study a nonlocal variational problem of Triebel-Lizorkin type.
AI.result: A compactness theorem for Sobolev spaces associated with ball Banach function spaces is established. A fractional Poincaré inequality in X(Ω) is proved with explicit dependence on (1-s)^{1/q}, independent of s and f. Well-posedness of a weighted Triebel-Lizorkin type nonlocal variational problem follows.
